title: Emit model metadata change events from control plane
preview: Context: The model metadata schema v1 work (registry + validation) needs a single, reliable stream of change events to drive the changelog generator and Console "change history" views. Today we have ad-hoc logging and some release-engineering notes, but not an auditable, machine-consumable event source. This PR introduces a canonical event envelope and emits two primary event types from the control plane whenever (a) model metadata is updated, and (b) a model version is promoted within an environment/deployment scope (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). It also implements idempotency + dedupe so consumers can safely replay. Summary of changes: 1) New event types and schema (v1): - redwood.model_registry.model_metadata_changed.v1 - redwood.model_registry.model_version_promoted.v1 Event envelope fields are consistent with our other platform events: event_id (UUID), occurred_at, emitted_at, actor (service/user), request_id, environment (prod/staging), and payload. Payload includes model_id, model_slug, variant_id (where applicable), metadata_schema_version, metadata_hash, and redaction_scope (public/internal). For promotion events, payload also includes from_version, to_version, rollout_policy_id (nullable), and deployment_scope (hosted|dedicated|private) + region (nullable). 2) Outbox-based delivery with deterministic idempotency keys: We write events into a control-plane outbox table in the same DB transaction as the metadata update/promotion (so we don't emit an event without the change). A background worker publishes to Event Bus (Kafka) and marks rows delivered. We compute a stable idempotency_key per event to prevent duplicates across retries and replays: - model_metadata_changed: sha256("model_metadata_changed:v1" + model_id + metadata_hash + public_or_internal + updated_at_rfc3339) - model_version_promoted: sha256("model_version_promoted:v1" + model_id + deployment_scope + region_or_all + to_version + promotion_id) This makes the publisher and downstream consumers (generator/Console) safe under at-least-once semantics. 3) Public vs internal separation: Events are emitted in two flavors when needed: a public event (public payload only) and an internal event (includes internal-only notes / eval references / operational details). The default bus topic is internal; public event forwarding is gated behind the changelog generator’s redaction filter and will be handled in the generator PR (see linked work). This PR ensures payloads are explicitly tagged with redaction_scope, and tests assert that public payloads never include internal_notes, jira_links, incident_refs, or raw eval artifacts. 4) Promotion semantics: Promotion events are emitted only when the "active" version pointer changes for a given scope (model+deployment_scope+region). Metadata edits that do not change the active version do not emit promotion events (only metadata_changed). Promotions caused by rollback emit the same event type with from_version/to_version swapped; the payload includes promotion_reason (manual|rollback|policy). 5) Observability + guardrails: - Metrics: control_plane_outbox_publish_total{event_type,status}, control_plane_outbox_backlog, control_plane_event_publish_latency_ms - Logging includes event_id, idempotency_key, request_id, and actor_id. - Feature flag: model_metadata_events_v1 enabled in staging by default; prod ramp via config. Notes for reviewers: - Event schema is defined in control-plane events module (protobuf-style struct + JSON serialization). The generator will consume JSON. We intentionally versioned the event name with .v1 to allow non-breaking schema evolution via .v2 later. - Some fields are currently nullable (rollout_policy_id, region) since not all promotion paths have that info yet. We should consider requiring rollout_policy_id once rollouts are fully wired (tracked in ENG-9823 follow-up). Related: - Depends on metadata hash computed by registry schema v1 (see redwood-model-registry PR #18421). - Migration/backfill PR #18429 will generate a small burst of metadata_changed events during backfill; this PR includes a backfill_suppression flag for the migration job to avoid spamming downstream consumers (default true for backfill job). Test plan: - Unit tests for idempotency_key stability and public/internal payload redaction - Integration test: metadata update transaction writes outbox row and publisher sends exactly once under retry - Integration test: promotion emits exactly one event per scope and is deduped across double-submit - Load test (staging): 10k events replay through publisher; no duplicate consumer processing when using idempotency_key as dedupe key. Rollout: - Land behind feature flag - Enable in staging (24h) and validate generator ingestion metrics - Enable in prod at 10%, 50%, 100% over 2 days; monitor outbox backlog and publish failures

title: Changelog generator: initial implementation + templates (public/internal filtering)
preview:
Summary: Introduces the initial version of the changelog generator used to produce customer-visible model release notes (Console + docs) and internal audit-focused notes from model metadata change events. This adds (1) an event ingestion layer (with idempotency), (2) category mapping + severity normalization, (3) a templating system aligned to the customer release notes taxonomy/style guide, and (4) strict public vs internal field filtering/redaction. Motivation: Today model/version changes are visible inconsistently across model registry, runtime config, and ad-hoc docs. Support escalations (e.g., SUP-28411) show we need a single, consistent change artifact generated from the same events that drive rollouts. This PR is the first end-to-end piece: given a stream of ModelMetadataChanged/ModelVersionPromoted events, produce deterministic changelog entries and expose them via an internal API consumed by Console and docs build. Scope: In scope: generator library + small service wrapper, templates, category mapping rules, redaction/publication rules, and test fixtures. Out of scope: full docs-site integration (tracked separately) and subscription notifications.

Key design points: Event-driven generation (per ADR-0143) with deterministic output. We generate an entry keyed by (model_id, model_version, event_id) and write it to ChangelogEntry storage. Reprocessing is safe (idempotent) and does not create duplicates. Public vs internal fields: The generator takes a ReleaseNoteVisibility policy (PUBLIC | INTERNAL) and filters fields accordingly. We default to PUBLIC for anything leaving the control plane boundary. Internal entries may include oncall hints and infra-only details, but MUST never be returned to non-admin Console roles. Category mapping: Maps raw changes into the standardized taxonomy: security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation, and other. Severity language is normalized (low/medium/high) and constrained for public notes (no unreviewed claims about quality improvements). Templates: Templating uses a small DSL (Go templates with a restricted function set) so we can update phrasing without code changes, while still keeping outputs structured (title, summary, impact, guidance links, metadata block).

Implementation details: New package: internal/changelog (core generation engine + mapping). New service: services/changelog-generator (HTTP + queue worker wrapper around the engine). Storage: adds changelog_entries table (or equivalent backing store) with fields for model_id, model_version, visibility, category, severity, rendered_markdown, rendered_json, source_event_id, and created_at. API: Internal endpoints: GET /internal/changelog/v1/entries?model_id=...&visibility=public|internal&limit=... and GET /internal/changelog/v1/entries/{entry_id}. These are intentionally internal-only; Console uses a separate backend-for-frontend endpoint that enforces RBAC (added in subsequent PR). Redaction: Applies allowlist-based field selection for PUBLIC output. Any unknown/internal-only fields are dropped. Additionally, we redact freeform strings if they match banned patterns (e.g., internal hostnames, incident links, ticket references) using a conservative sanitizer. Tests: Adds fixtures for common change events: version promotion, tokenizer/runtime bump, quantization profile change, deprecation window change, and security patch. Includes golden tests for rendered outputs to prevent template drift.

Review/rollout notes: This PR is intended to land behind an internal feature flag changelog_generator_v1. After merge, we will dogfood by generating entries for a small subset of models in staging, then enable writes in prod with READ-only consumers initially. Follow-ups will wire Console UI and docs generation to the API, and add CI checks for preview artifacts.

Manual testing: Ran worker locally with recorded events from staging; verified deterministic output and redaction behavior. Verified that PUBLIC output contains no internal links/tickets and that INTERNAL output preserves operator notes.

Docs: Template wording and categories align with the customer release notes taxonomy/style guide; any future wording changes should occur in template files + golden tests.

Risk: Primary risk is accidentally leaking internal information in PUBLIC output. Mitigations: strict allowlist, sanitizer, golden tests, and a required security review on template changes.

Screenshots: N/A (service/library).

title: Tag eval runs with model version + model-metadata schema hash
preview: Context: As part of the Model Metadata Schema v1 + automated changelog work, we need to be able to trace a customer-visible release note entry back to concrete eval evidence (and vice versa). Today the eval harness records the model identifier and some run params, but does not persist (a) the promoted model version string used for serving (e.g., catalog version/variant) and (b) the model-metadata schema hash that the changelog generator references. That makes it hard to answer support questions like “which exact model version did this eval run validate?” and blocks automated linking in release notes. What this PR does: 1) Adds required fields to the eval run manifest/results payload: model_version (string) and model_metadata_schema_hash (sha256 hex). 2) Populates these fields from the run config when available, and falls back to resolving via the model registry client (best-effort) when the caller provides model_id but not a version. 3) Propagates the tags to: run.json, summary.json, and the top-level artifact index so downstream tooling (changelog generator / Console) can join on (model_version, schema_hash) without parsing logs. 4) Adds CLI flags + env var support: --model-version, --model-metadata-schema-hash, with REDWOOD_MODEL_VERSION / REDWOOD_MODEL_METADATA_SCHEMA_HASH overrides (CI-friendly). 5) Adds validation: schema hash must be 64-char hex; model_version must be non-empty and match our semver-ish format (allows catalog suffixes like "+q4_k_m"). Compatibility: Existing stored runs remain readable; the reader treats missing fields as "unknown" and emits a warning (no hard failure). New runs will include the fields. Testing: unit tests for validation + serialization; integration test that runs a tiny harness workflow and asserts artifacts contain tags; golden-file update for run manifest. Rollout notes: we should update the CI templates in redwood-model-registry and the internal eval job definitions to pass the schema hash (follow-up in ENG-9827 checklist).

title: Add Private observability smoke-test harness (kind + Helm) and gate on core signals
preview:
Motivation: We keep finding regressions in Private installs where Prometheus scrapes succeed but dashboards/rules don’t render (missing ConfigMaps), or metrics rename breaks the minimum pack. This PR adds a deterministic smoke-test harness that deploys a minimal Private stack into a fresh kind cluster and asserts the observability minimum pack is actually usable (scrapes + basic PromQL + dashboards + alert rules). This is intended to be the v1 gate for ENG-19156 and to prevent repeats of INT-11804 failures and SUP-49210-style issues.

What this adds: (1) `hack/private_observability_smoke/` harness that creates a kind cluster, installs dependencies (kube-prometheus-stack + grafana), installs Redwood Private via Helm (control-plane + gateway + one runtime deployment), then validates signals; (2) a Go-based validator binary that queries Prometheus HTTP API and checks for presence of expected time series and non-empty results; (3) a CI job that runs on PRs touching Helm/installer/metrics/observability codepaths; (4) documentation in-repo for running locally with tunables for air-gapped-ish constraints (image overrides, no external chart repos).

Validation scope (minimum supported): - Prometheus target discovery: Redwood pods appear in `/api/v1/targets` and are `up==1` - Golden signals: `redwood_request_total`, `redwood_request_errors_total`, and `redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket` (or recording-rule equivalents) are present and queryable - Runtime health signals: GPU metrics are not required in kind; we validate runtime queue depth + token throughput metrics if exported, but do not fail if GPU exporter is disabled - Dashboards/rules packaging: expected Grafana dashboard ConfigMaps exist and are labeled for the sidecar; PrometheusRule resources exist and load without parse errors.

Implementation notes: - Harness is idempotent and cleans up the kind cluster on exit; also supports `--keep-cluster` for debugging. - We pin kind node image and chart versions to reduce flake. - We avoid relying on external DNS by using port-forward to Prometheus/Grafana and querying over localhost. - Default timeouts were increased (cold start + image pulls) and we added bounded retries around target readiness.

Example validator checks (illustrative): `GET http://127.0.0.1:9090/api/v1/query?query=sum(rate(redwood_request_total[2m]))` must return a non-empty vector after generating a small amount of traffic against the gateway. We generate traffic via a minimal in-cluster job that hits `/v1/chat/completions` with a tiny prompt and expects HTTP 200.

Out of scope: full e2e quality evals, performance baselines, GPU exporter validation, and customer-specific sinks (Splunk/ELK). This is a smoke test for “signals show up” and “packaging isn’t broken.”

Follow-ups: - Add a second lane that runs with network policies enabled to catch scrape regressions earlier (related to incident thread about tightened NetworkPolicies). - Add a nightly job with a larger retention window to catch TSDB churn/memory regressions.

Checklist: - [x] New harness runnable locally (README) - [x] CI job added + scoped path filters - [x] Flake mitigation (pinned versions, retries, cleanup) - [x] Runbook link placeholders in alert annotations are not validated here (handled in observability-pack PRs) - [x] No secrets or payload content logged by harness (only status codes + request ids).

title: Standardize metric labels and reduce cardinality for Private installs
preview: Context: Private installs run customer-managed Prometheus with tighter TSDB/storage limits and stricter cardinality constraints than our hosted environments. We observed high series churn from per-request and per-route/model labels, especially when customers have many routes, frequently changing model versions, or multi-tenant traffic. This PR standardizes label names across control plane + data plane services and introduces bounded label strategies for Private (while keeping a compatibility path for existing dashboards/alerts). Scope: (1) Label normalization: unify common label keys across services (service/component/namespace/cluster), rename inconsistent keys (route vs endpoint, model vs model_name), and remove labels that should never exist on metrics (request_id, raw path). (2) Cardinality reduction for Private: replace unbounded labels with bounded dimensions: route_group (config-defined, limited set), model_family (derived, bounded), tenant_bucket (optional, hashed bucket count), and status_class (2xx/4xx/5xx). (3) Compatibility: dual-emit old + new metrics/labels for a deprecation window behind a feature flag, plus recording rules that map old queries to new series so dashboards/alerts keep working. (4) Documentation: add guidance in the Private metrics standards doc and inline comments explaining why certain labels are disallowed. Implementation details: - Added a shared metrics helper package used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime sidecars to enforce label allowlists and cap label values. - Introduced `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_METRICS_MODE=bounded` (default on for Private Helm values; off by default in hosted/dev) to enable bounding behavior. - For route dimension: emit `route_group` (from route config; defaults to "default"; max 50 distinct values per cluster enforced with "other" overflow). - For model dimension: emit `model_family` (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen; unknown -> "other") and `model_variant` (optional; capped to top-N by request volume per process; overflow -> "other"). - For tenant dimension: do not emit raw tenant_id; emit `tenant_bucket` only when enabled (default disabled) to support coarse multi-tenant skew debugging without exploding series. - Removed `path` label usage from HTTP metrics in gateway; replaced with `route_group` + `handler` (bounded). - Updated recording rules used by the minimum pack dashboards to prefer the new labels, while still supporting legacy selectors. Notes: This is a stepping stone for the Private observability minimum pack (dashboards/alerts). Keeping dual emission avoids a hard cutover, but we should schedule removal once the minimum pack GA branch is stable. Testing: - Unit tests assert label allowlist enforcement and overflow behavior. - Added a micro-benchmark for the label bounding map to ensure we don’t introduce hot-path locks. - Verified in the private smoke-test harness that series count stays within expected limits on a synthetic 200-route / 80-model workload. Rollout plan: - Enable bounded mode by default for Private Helm chart values (separate PR in charts). - Keep legacy labels available for 2 releases; emit a log warning in bounded mode when legacy selectors are detected (dashboard queries) only in debug builds (no customer-facing noise). - Document migration in `docs/private/observability/metrics-migrations.md` and link in alert annotations where relevant.

(2) Cardinality reduction for Private: replace unbounded labels with bounded dimensions: route_group (config-defined, limited set), model_family (derived, bounded), tenant_bucket (optional, hashed bucket count), and status_class (2xx/4xx/5xx). (3) Compatibility: dual-emit old + new metrics/labels for a deprecation window behind a feature flag, plus recording rules that map old queries to new series so dashboards/alerts keep working. (4) Documentation: add guidance in the Private metrics standards doc and inline comments explaining why certain labels are disallowed. Implementation details: - Added a shared metrics helper package used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime sidecars to enforce label allowlists and cap label values. - Introduced `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_METRICS_MODE=bounded` (default on for Private Helm values; off by default in hosted/dev) to enable bounding behavior. - For route dimension: emit `route_group` (from route config; defaults to "default"; max 50 distinct values per cluster enforced with "other" overflow). - For model dimension: emit `model_family` (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen; unknown -> "other") and `model_variant` (optional; capped to top-N by request volume per process; overflow -> "other"). - For tenant dimension: do not emit raw tenant_id; emit `tenant_bucket` only when enabled (default disabled) to support coarse multi-tenant skew debugging without exploding series. - Removed `path` label usage from HTTP metrics in gateway; replaced with `route_group` + `handler` (bounded). - Updated recording rules used by the minimum pack dashboards to prefer the new labels, while still supporting legacy selectors. Notes: This is a stepping stone for the Private observability minimum pack (dashboards/alerts). Keeping dual emission avoids a hard cutover, but we should schedule removal once the minimum pack GA branch is stable. Testing: - Unit tests assert label allowlist enforcement and overflow behavior. - Added a micro-benchmark for the label bounding map to ensure we don’t introduce hot-path locks. - Verified in the private smoke-test harness that series count stays within expected limits on a synthetic 200-route / 80-model workload. Rollout plan: - Enable bounded mode by default for Private Helm chart values (separate PR in charts). - Keep legacy labels available for 2 releases; emit a log warning in bounded mode when legacy selectors are detected (dashboard queries) only in debug builds (no customer-facing noise). - Document migration in `docs/private/observability/metrics-migrations.md` and link in alert annotations where relevant.

title: Dedicated autoscaler: add metrics + structured logs for desired vs bounded capacity and prediction error
preview:
Context: As part of Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode, we need auditability and operability around what the autoscaler *wanted* to do vs what it actually did after applying customer bounds (min/max) and safety invariants. This PR adds first-class metrics and structured logs for (1) requested desired replicas vs bounded desired replicas, (2) bound-hit counters (clamped to min/max), (3) prediction error and staleness when predictive mode is enabled, and (4) an explicit scale-event reason enum that shows up in both metrics and logs.

What’s in this PR:
- New gauge metrics (emitted every autoscaler tick per deployment/region):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.desired_replicas (the raw desired output from the decision function)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bounded_desired_replicas (after applying min/max + safety floors)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.current_replicas
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.queue_depth (existing signal re-emitted consistently for dashboards)
- New counters:
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bound_hit_total{bound="min"|"max"} increments when desired is clamped
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.scale_event_total{direction="up"|"down", reason=*} increments on actual scale actions
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_stale_total increments when a forecast is ignored due to staleness
- New histograms (guarded by config to avoid extra cardinality):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_error_ratio (|predicted - actual| / max(actual,1))
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.decision_latency_ms (internal time to compute desired)
- Structured decision logs (one per tick) with stable field names for searchability and compliance reviews. Key fields:
  - deployment_id, customer_id, region
  - policy_min, policy_max, predictive_enabled
  - desired_replicas, bounded_desired_replicas, current_replicas
  - scale_action (none|up|down), scale_delta
  - reason (enum), reason_detail (short string)
  - predictor_used (true/false), predictor_age_seconds

Scale reason enum (used consistently across metric label + log field):
- reactive_queue_depth
- reactive_saturation
- predictive_forecast
- bounded_by_max
- bounded_by_min
- cooldown_active
- no_change
- invalid_prediction

Cardinality / labeling:
- All metrics are labeled with deployment_id and region. customer_id is included only in structured logs (NOT as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality blowups. This matches the current fleet metrics policy.
- reason labels are limited to the enum above (no freeform strings).

Illustrative example (structured log):
{"msg":"autoscaler_tick","deployment_id":"ded-9d2f...","region":"us-east-1","policy_min":4,"policy_max":24,"predictive_enabled":true,"desired_replicas":31,"bounded_desired_replicas":24,"current_replicas":20,"scale_action":"up","scale_delta":4,"reason":"bounded_by_max","reason_detail":"desired=31 clamped to max=24","predictor_used":true,"predictor_age_seconds":58}

Tests:
- Unit tests for reason selection (predictive vs reactive overrides) to ensure we don’t emit conflicting reason labels.
- Integration-style test for clamp behavior to ensure bound_hit_total increments exactly once per tick when clamping occurs.

Follow-ups (out of this PR):
- Grafana dashboard panels + alert rules live in observability-pack PRs.
- Control plane audit event schema (who/when/what) is handled in the audit-events PR.

Rollout:
- Metrics are behind the existing dedicated_autoscaling_bounds feature gate. When disabled, no new bounded/predictive metrics are emitted.
- Prediction histograms default off in prod until we validate cost/volume during pilot (config: autoscaler.emit_prediction_histograms=false).

Refs: ENG-22143, ENG-22140. Also informs debugging for SUP-28479 (pilot oscillation) by exposing scale_event reasons and predictor staleness.

title: Export benchmark results as versioned CSV/JSON artifacts for web ingestion (schema + changelog)
preview:
Context: Marketing site + interactive benchmark table needs a stable, versioned export of benchmark results that can be ingested by the web build pipeline and served via the results service (ENG-18566). This PR adds an export step that materializes benchmark outputs into (1) a canonical JSON bundle and (2) a CSV for quick inspection / spreadsheet workflows. Both artifacts are versioned, have an explicit schema version, and include a machine-readable changelog so the docs/web layer can safely detect and adapt to changes.

What’s included: - New exporter module (export/public_artifacts) that takes validated run aggregates and emits:   - artifacts/public/v1/results.json (canonical)   - artifacts/public/v1/results.csv (flat, human-friendly)   - artifacts/public/v1/changelog.json (schema + field-level changes)   - artifacts/public/v1/manifest.json (build metadata: git sha, created_at, prompt_set, regions, models, filters) - Schema versioning:   - schema_version follows semver and is embedded in every exported file header/metadata.   - Current version: 1.0.0 - JSON schema file added (schemas/public_results.schema.json) for internal validation + downstream contract tests. - Stable ordering and determinism: exporter sorts rows by (model_id, region, workload_id, context_bucket) and emits canonical JSON with stable key ordering to reduce diff noise in PRs and caching layers. - Backfill-friendly: exporter supports including a "dataset_id" and "run_group_id" so we can publish multiple refreshes without clobbering prior runs. - CLI changes: benchmark-lab runner adds --export-public-artifacts and --public-artifacts-dir flags; default path is ./artifacts/public/v1. - Tests: unit tests for schema compliance, CSV column consistency, and changelog generation; golden-file snapshot tests for results.json and results.csv. - Docs: README section on "Public artifacts" including intended consumers (docs site, marketing site, results service) and guidance for bumping schema_version.

Non-goals / guardrails: - No changes to how metrics are computed (latency/$ normalization stays in PR-4821). This is only an export + contract layer. - No PII / sensitive IDs are included; this PR intentionally only exports aggregated results and high-level config descriptors. (Redaction enforcement is handled separately; see follow-up PR-4873.)

Consumer contract (high level): - results.json includes: schema_version, dataset_id, generated_at, dimensions (model/region/workload/context_bucket), metrics (p50/p95 latency_ms, throughput_toks_per_s, cost_per_1m_tokens_usd, latency_per_dollar), and method flags (streaming=false for this dataset, caching_policy_id, batching_policy_id). - results.csv is a 1-row-per-dimension record intended for quick charting; it shares metric names with results.json where possible.

Rollout plan: - Land exporter behind the CLI flag; CI will run exports on the small smoke dataset. - Web team (Felix) will consume artifacts from a pinned schema_version and fail fast if schema_version mismatches expected major version. - Once docs site wiring is merged, we will enable exports in the full region sweep pipeline.

Risks: - Schema churn during methodology tweaks. Mitigation: semver schema_version + changelog.json + downstream contract tests. - Size of JSON bundle if we add too many slices. Mitigation: exporter supports filtering dimensions and splitting outputs per region/model in a follow-up if needed.

title: Rollout canary safety smoke tests (cohorting + auto-stop wiring) for staging
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a small suite of staging-only safety smoke tests for Hosted canary rollouts. The goal is to catch two classes of beta issues before they reach customers: (1) cohorting / split enforcement regressions (e.g., observed split mismatch per region) and (2) stop-condition wiring regressions (e.g., SLO breach evaluates but orchestrator doesn’t pause/rollback, or rollout step stalls on metric timeouts). These tests are intentionally narrow, deterministic, and cleanup-safe so they can be run as part of GA change management.

Motivation / context: During beta we saw an incident-like split mismatch in us-east (SUP-48377) and a separate class of failures where metric evaluation timeouts could stall progress (see follow-ups in ENG-9158 / pr-28895). We already have E2E coverage in the monorepo (ENG-9164), but we wanted a lightweight “canary of the canary” that runs against staging infrastructure end-to-end (routing + telemetry aggregation + orchestrator actions) without needing full Console UI flows.

What’s included: 1) Cohorting correctness smoke: Creates a temporary Hosted route + rollout with baseline/canary config, sets split to 20% canary, and sends N=2,000 synthetic requests with a fixed cohorting key distribution. Validates: (a) per-request cohort assignment is stable for the same key across retries, (b) overall split converges to expected bounds (20% ± 3% at N=2,000), (c) split is consistent across regions when the same key is used (us-east-1 + eu-west-1), and (d) streaming requests retain cohort stickiness across reconnect within the allowed retry window.

2) Stop-condition wiring smoke: Starts a rollout step and intentionally injects failures into the canary cohort only (via the staging failure-injection header understood by the Hosted gateway) to exceed the default stop threshold for 5xx within a short window. Validates: (a) telemetry tags include rollout_id and cohort and are queryable via the staging metrics API, (b) stop-condition evaluator fires and emits an action event, (c) orchestrator transitions the rollout into PAUSED or ROLLED_BACK per policy (configurable per test), and (d) no orphaned rollouts remain after test completion.

3) Anti-cardinality + safety rails: Tests do not create unbounded (rollout_id, route, region) series. The suite uses a single route per run with a unique suffix, but aggregates assertions via the orchestrator + a single metrics query path. Added guardrails that hard-fail if route names exceed a maximum length or if run attempts to create more than 2 rollouts.

4) Cleanup and time-bounding: Every test uses a strict max runtime with deferred cleanup. If a run times out, we still attempt to rollback/delete the rollout and delete the staging route. Cleanup is idempotent and safe to re-run.

Implementation details: - Uses the perf-canary harness to call control-plane and orchestrator endpoints directly (no Console dependency). - Adds a small wrapper for deterministic key generation to validate hash cohorting behavior. - Adds a metrics polling helper with exponential backoff and a hard cap; avoids busy-waiting. - Uses a single staging-only feature flag gate so the job is a no-op in non-staging environments.

Changed areas (high level): - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_split_accuracy.ts: new split validation test and cohort stickiness assertions - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_stop_condition.ts: new stop-condition wiring test with failure injection - lib/staging/orchestrator_client.ts: helper for rollout create/start/pause/rollback + state polling - lib/staging/metrics_client.ts: helper for cohort-tagged metrics queries with bounded polling - lib/staging/cleanup.ts: idempotent cleanup for routes + rollouts - ci/nightly.yml and ci/on_demand.yml: new job definition + required secrets

Illustrative snippet (cohort stickiness assertion): The test tracks (cohort, request_id) for a stable key and asserts that retries do not flip cohorts unless the rollout config changed. This mirrors the ADR decision for deterministic cohorting and protects against accidental inclusion of unstable fields in the hash input.

How to run: - On demand (staging): ./scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh --suite rollouts --env staging - CI: job “staging-rollout-smoke” (nightly) and optional manual dispatch

Rollout / risk: Low risk to production since this is perf-canary only. Risk to staging is mitigated via strict cleanup and caps. If staging is degraded, the suite is expected to fail fast and page nobody (non-paging); it will file a CI failure for visibility.

Follow-ups: - Consider adding a latency regression stop-condition test once default thresholds are finalized (ENG-9158). - Consider adding a “split mismatch per AZ” probe once we have better visibility into the per-AZ routing logs.

Checklist: - [x] Tests are time-bounded and cleanup-safe - [x] Does not increase metrics cardinality unboundedly - [x] Validates both routing enforcement and telemetry stop-condition wiring - [x] Documented required staging secrets - [x] Linked to relevant Linear + support ticket

title: Add e2e integration tests with Splunk HEC + Datadog Logs sandboxes
preview: Context: As part of SIEM-ready audit export beta, we need repeatable end-to-end confidence that the exporter can deliver to downstreams with realistic failure modes (auth errors, 429 throttling, payload validation issues) and that our retry/backoff + checkpointing semantics behave as expected. This PR adds an integration test suite + docker-compose-ish harness that boots the exporter alongside (a) a Splunk-like HEC stub service and (b) a Datadog Logs intake stub service. It does not attempt to fully emulate Splunk/Datadog; it’s focused on request/response behavior the adapters depend on. Scope: - New `e2e/` harness with compose config for local + CI runs - Splunk HEC stub: validates token header, supports `/services/collector/event` + `/services/collector/health`, can simulate 429s and partial failures - Datadog stub: validates API key header, supports `/api/v2/logs`, can simulate 403 (bad key), 429 (rate limit), and payload schema errors - E2E tests that drive synthetic audit events through the exporter pipeline and assert: (1) delivery occurs, (2) retry/backoff on 429, (3) idempotency/dedup expectations at the destination boundary, (4) metrics emitted (lag, retries, adapter errors) - CI wiring: add a gated job that runs on PRs touching adapters/export pipeline; run time ~6–8 min on standard runners Notes/limitations: - The stubs persist received events to a local volume and expose a `/received` endpoint for assertions. - Tests are intentionally strict about headers/content-type/encoding because we’ve seen regressions around compression and batching. - This suite assumes the existing local dev harness can inject synthetic audit events into the exporter (see `scripts/dev/seed_audit_events.ts`); the test wrapper calls it directly. - No real Splunk/Datadog containers are used in CI; those are flaky and require extra licensing/credentials. Related: ENG-5195. Follow-ups: (1) add S3 path to harness (separate PR), (2) add chaos mode for long downstream outage + auto-pause behavior (tracked in ENG-1119 follow-up work).

title: Add Audit Exporter Grafana dashboards + alert rules (lag, delivery errors)
preview: Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we need first-class operational visibility for the audit-log-exporter service (lag, retries, adapter failures) and alerting aligned to the SLOs defined by SRE. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for the exporter, intended to be deployed to hosted + dedicated environments for the beta cohort. Links: Linear ENG-5168 (exporter observability), Confluence SLO doc: "Audit exporter SLOs and alerting" (owner: Sean/Rafael). Scope: (1) Grafana dashboard: Overview (lag, throughput, success rate), (2) Grafana dashboard: Destination adapters (Splunk HEC / Datadog / S3), (3) Alert rules: export lag burn, sustained adapter errors, DLQ growth, auto-paused exports, (4) Recording rules for rate-based panels to reduce query cost. Notes on semantics: Export lag is computed from exporter checkpoint watermark vs event time; alerts use p95 lag by tenant and also a global max with cardinality controls. We intentionally avoid alerting on single-tenant spikes unless they violate the global budget or a configured "beta tenant allowlist" label is present. Rollout: safe to merge; rules are gated behind the existing audit-exporter job selector and will be enabled per cluster by SRE during beta rollout.

title: Add canary for telemetry query latency (incident-bot diagnostics guardrail)
preview:
## Context
Incident-bot v2 relies on the telemetry query service to render saturation/context cards in the first ~60–90s of an incident thread. We’ve seen query latency spikes during incidents (bursty usage + upstream timeouts) that cause empty/partial cards and slow responder workflows. This PR adds a dedicated perf canary to continuously validate telemetry query latency and error behavior under a representative diagnostic workload.

Related:
- INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts from incident-bot)
- ENG-5427 (telemetry query cache/coalescing)
- ENG-5429 (bot degraded-mode + load shedding)

## What this adds
1) New canary: `telemetry-query-latency`
- Runs every 60s (configurable) from 3 regions: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1
- Executes a fixed set of low-cardinality queries that mirror incident-bot cards:
  - GPU utilization (pool-level, region scoped)
  - Queue depth / scheduler backpressure (service + region)
  - KV cache pressure (cluster + region)
  - Error budget burn link generator preflight query (metadata only)
- Includes a small amount of concurrency (default 4) to catch p95 regressions without introducing significant load

2) Success criteria + SLO-style thresholds
- Records latency histograms for each query family + overall request
- Enforces guardrails:
  - p95 < 2.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - p99 < 5.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - timeout rate < 1% over 10m
  - non-2xx rate < 0.5% over 10m
- Canary marks a run as failed if any threshold is violated; emits a structured failure reason to make debugging actionable (e.g., `upstream_timeout`, `rate_limited`, `bad_gateway`, `query_budget_exceeded`).

3) Metrics + dashboard wiring
Emits the following (Prom-style) metrics:
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_latency_ms{region,query_family,tenant_scope}` (histogram)
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_success_total{region,query_family}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_failure_total{region,query_family,reason}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_payload_bytes{region,query_family}` (to detect accidental query expansion)

Adds a lightweight dashboard panel set (documented in the runbook) and a deep-link format compatible with incident-bot cards.

4) Alert
Adds an alert intended for SRE + telemetry oncall:
- Fires when p95 or p99 breaches for 10m OR when timeout rate breaches for 10m
- Includes annotations for top failing query_family and region
- Routes to `#eng-sre` and `#telemetry-oncall` (same routing as other perf canaries)

## Implementation details
- Canary uses the same auth mechanism as other perf canaries, but is scoped to a synthetic service identity with read-only access to the telemetry query endpoint. No tenant/customer identifiers are used.
- Query parameters are explicitly bounded (time window 5m, region scoped, no per-tenant grouping) to avoid creating cardinality pressure.
- Adds backoff + jitter when a run fails (prevents thundering herd if telemetry service is unhealthy).
- Adds an allowlist of query templates to prevent accidental addition of ad-hoc expensive queries.

## Changed areas
- `canaries/telemetry_query_latency_canary.ts`: new canary implementation + query families
- `canaries/index.ts`: registry wiring
- `config/canaries.yaml`: scheduling + regions + thresholds
- `alerts/telemetry_query_latency.rules.yaml`: alert definitions
- `runbooks/telemetry-query-latency-canary.md`: triage steps and escalation pointers
- `dashboards/telemetry_query_latency_canary.json`: panels for latency/errors by region and query family

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - threshold evaluation and failure reason mapping
  - query template rendering and hard bounds (time window, grouping)
  - retry/backoff behavior
- Local dry-run: `yarn canary telemetry-query-latency --once --region us-east-1`

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind `canary.telemetryQueryLatency.enabled` (default off)
- Enable in staging for 24h, then prod in us-east-1 only for 24h
- Expand to remaining regions after confirming alert noise and baseline distributions

## Notes
This canary is intentionally conservative: it aims to detect when incident-bot context gathering is at risk, not to load test the telemetry query service. If we want load-based validation, we should add a separate stress canary with an explicit budget and ownership.

---
### Commits
- canary: add telemetry query latency canary scaffold
- canary: implement bounded query families (gpu/queue/kv/error-budget)
- metrics: add histograms + failure reason labels
- alerts: add telemetry query latency SLO alert
- docs: add runbook + dashboard references

### CI
- build (node 20): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- canary dry-run (staging): pass

### Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

---
### Review conversation highlights
Logan Wright: "Please double-check that the canary can’t accidentally introduce high-cardinality tags (tenant_id, model, route). We’ve been burned here." 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added a hard allowlist for group-by keys and a unit test that fails if `tenant_id|model|route` appear in the rendered query. Also capped time window to 5m."

Allison Grant: "Alert will be noisy if we page on single-region transient spikes. Can we require 2/3 regions OR a 10m window?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Updated rule to 10m and added annotations for top region/query_family; kept per-region firing but with a 10m window and higher p99 threshold to reduce flaps."

Miles Thornton: "Can we include a hint in the alert on what incident-bot symptom this maps to?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added alert annotation: 'Incident-bot diagnostics may degrade (empty/slow context cards). Consider enabling degraded-mode (links-only) if widespread.'"

Caleb Johnson: "Any risk that this canary masks real issues because it uses cache?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "We explicitly set `cache_bypass=false` to match incident-bot behavior; the goal is end-user perceived latency. Added metric for payload_bytes + noted in runbook how to check uncached latency if debugging."

title: Add runbook links to experiment alerts + Console guardrail banners
preview: Problem: During beta we saw multiple slow triage loops where the on-call (and Support) had to ask around for the right runbook when an experiment guardrail alert fired (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant skew, etc.). We already have runbooks in Confluence, but the link isn’t present where the incident starts: alert payloads and the Console guardrail banners. This PR wires canonical runbook URLs into (1) alert payload annotations (so PagerDuty/Slack notifications carry a direct ‘Runbook’ link) and (2) Console guardrail banners on the experiment details/results pages. Scope: route experiments only (no global SRE alert conventions refactor in this PR). What changed: - Added a small, versioned mapping of {alert_key -> runbook_url} for experiment-related alerts (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant 0 traffic, allocation skew, p99 regression auto-stop). - Plumbed runbook_url through the alert/notification payload used by the guardrail evaluator and the notifications pipeline. - Updated Console guardrail banner model to accept an optional runbook_url and render a ‘View runbook’ CTA when present. - Added safeguards: if an alert key is unknown or the URL is missing/invalid, we omit the link (no broken UI, no malformed payload). - Added unit tests for mapping + URL validation and an integration test that asserts the runbook link is present end-to-end for a representative guardrail event. Runbook sources (canonical): - Experiment assignment debugging runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Assignment+Debugging+Runbook - Experiment health dashboards spec (links out to dashboards/runbooks): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Health+Dashboards+Spec Notes: - This intentionally does not change Grafana rule definitions in observability-pack; it only ensures Redwood-originated notifications + Console surfaces carry the runbook URL. (Observability-pack PRs can independently add runbook_url annotations at rule level.) - URL allowlist: limited to confluence.redwoodinference.com to avoid arbitrary links in alert payloads. - Console UI is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; the banner rendering is a no-op if the backend doesn’t send runbook_url. Testing: - Unit: GuardrailAlertRunbookMapTest, UrlValidationTest - Integration: ExperimentGuardrailNotificationE2E (asserts runbook_url in notification payload + Console API response) Rollout: safe to ship with no config migrations; if we need to change runbook URLs, we can update the mapping and redeploy. Follow-ups: - Add runbook_url annotation in observability-pack alert rules so Grafana-native alerts match Redwood notifications. - Expand the mapping to cover non-experiment Rollouts alerts after GA if this proves useful.

title: Add exporter metrics (lag/retries/throughput), basic tracing, and structured logs
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-pass Prometheus metrics + basic tracing + structured logging to the audit-log-exporter service. Metrics are aligned to Redwood observability naming/cardinality standards (per Allison/Logan feedback) and provide the signals needed for initial dashboards/alerts (lag, throughput, retries, adapter errors). This work is scoped to exporter-side instrumentation only; the control plane status endpoint will consume these signals via existing health paths and the observability pack dashboards will be done separately. Context: ENG-5168. Motivation: During beta soak we’ve been debugging exporter lag spikes and downstream 429/backpressure mostly via ad-hoc logs. We need stable metrics to (a) quantify export lag vs SLO, (b) separate downstream throttling from internal queueing, (c) support paging policy (page on sustained lag / hard failures, ticket on transient retries), and (d) give Support/CS a consistent way to answer “are we exporting?” Implementation details (metrics): Adds a dedicated Prometheus registry for the service and standardizes metric names under the audit exporter namespace. Key metrics: 1) redwood_audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds (gauge): computed as now() - last_successful_checkpoint_timestamp, exported per destination_type and tenant_tier only (no tenant_id/export_config_id labels). 2) redwood_audit_exporter_events_exported_total (counter): increments per event successfully handed off to an adapter (post-filtering), labels: destination_type, result=success|retryable_error|fatal_error. 3) redwood_audit_exporter_batches_exported_total (counter): increments per batch attempt, labels: destination_type, result=success|retryable_error|fatal_error, http_status_class=2xx|4xx|5xx|none (where applicable). 4) redwood_audit_exporter_batch_send_duration_seconds (histogram): adapter send latency, labels: destination_type, result; buckets tuned to cover typical HEC/Datadog/S3 behavior (including a long tail for throttling scenarios). 5) redwood_audit_exporter_retries_total (counter) and redwood_audit_exporter_retry_backoff_seconds (histogram): records retry loop activity and backoff durations. 6) redwood_audit_exporter_dlq_batches_total (counter) and redwood_audit_exporter_dlq_depth (gauge): DLQ health signals. 7) redwood_audit_exporter_config_sync_success_total / _failure_total (counters) and redwood_audit_exporter_config_sync_age_seconds (gauge): config sync observability. Notes on naming/cardinality: Per observability standards, labels avoid high-cardinality identifiers (tenant UUID, export_config_id, destination host). Those identifiers are emitted in structured logs only. destination_type is one of: splunk_hec|datadog_logs|s3. tenant_tier is derived from config (hosted|dedicated) for now; private is not expected in beta. Implementation details (tracing): Adds OpenTelemetry spans around the exporter batch lifecycle (fetch -> filter -> adapt/send -> checkpoint). Span names follow Redwood conventions: audit_exporter.fetch, audit_exporter.filter, audit_exporter.adapter.send, audit_exporter.checkpoint.write. Attributes include destination_type, batch_size_events, and retry_attempt (bounded). Trace context is propagated through internal goroutines; adapter HTTP clients now attach trace headers where applicable. Tracing is intentionally lightweight (no per-event spans) to avoid overhead and cardinality explosions. Implementation details (structured logs): Switches exporter logs to a consistent JSON structure with stable keys: service=audit-log-exporter, component=exporter|adapter|checkpoint|config_sync, destination_type, export_stream=primary, attempt, batch_id (opaque UUID), and error_kind (classification for retry/backoff policies). tenant_id/workspace_id are included only at INFO for control-plane initiated operations and at DEBUG for hot paths; by default we log export_config_id only when errors occur (to help CS/SRE triage) and we explicitly do not log payload/event bodies. Also adds log redaction safeguards for credential fields (ensures tokens/keys never render via fmt/%+v). Testing: - Unit tests for metric label validation and histogram bucket sanity. - Updated adapter tests to assert that errors are classified consistently (retryable vs fatal) and emit the right counters. - Manual: ran local harness with Splunk/Datadog stubs and confirmed metrics scrape at /metrics, spans in local collector, and logs include trace_id/span_id. Ops/rollout: Metrics are behind the existing service config flag observability.metrics_enabled (default true in staging, false in local unless explicitly enabled). Tracing is gated by OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT presence; if not set, spans are no-op. No changes to request processing semantics. Follow-ups: - Add dashboards/alerts in observability-pack (separate PR). - Revisit export_lag_seconds definition when we add per-destination checkpoint streams (currently one checkpoint per destination config). - Confirm alert thresholds with SRE once we have beta traffic baselines. Changelog/notes: No API changes. No customer-facing behavior change beyond improved operational visibility.

title: Add production readiness quickstart aligned with Console onboarding checklist
preview: This adds a new end-to-end “Production Readiness” quickstart intended to mirror the Console onboarding / first production launch checklist. The quickstart walks a new Hosted API user from: API key setup → first request → streaming → retries/timeouts → usage visibility → quotas/budgets guardrails → basic observability hooks → model pinning + fallback (conceptual; uses config placeholders). Motivation: we keep seeing early prod launches fail on 401/403 setup mistakes, streaming disconnect confusion, and 429 surprises; the checklist in Console helps, but we need a canonical runnable path customers can copy/paste and deploy. title: Add onboarding KPIs dashboard (TTFS/TTFP + checklist funnel) and recommended alerts
preview:
## Summary
Adds a packaged Grafana dashboard + alert rules for the Guided Onboarding / First Production Launch initiative.

This is intended to be imported via the standard `observability-pack` rollout (same mechanism as the latency/cost packs), and targets the onboarding telemetry datasets produced by the gateway/control plane eventing work + derived milestone tables.

## What’s included
Dashboard: **Onboarding KPIs (Hosted API)**
- TTFS (signup → first successful 2xx request) trend
  - Median / p90 / p95 by signup cohort
  - Breakdown by `sdk_language` (via user-agent tags), `region`, and `plan_tier`
- TTFP (signup → first production traffic) trend
  - Median / p90 by signup cohort
  - Includes optional filter for "prod_traffic" classifier (see notes below)
- Step completion funnel
  - % of tenants completing each checklist step (ordered)
  - Step-to-next-step conversion + time-in-step
  - Top drop-off steps (ranked)
- Drop-off reasons / first failure signals
  - First auth failure (401/403) rate by cohort
  - First 429 rate by cohort
  - First streaming disconnect signal rate (from gateway termination reasons)
- Data quality tiles
  - Event ingest freshness (minutes behind)
  - Missing required dimensions rate (null/unknown sdk_language/region)

Alerts (recommended; shipped disabled-by-default)
- **Onboarding KPI dataset freshness**
  - Triggers when `onboarding_events_v1` ingestion is > 30m behind for 2 consecutive evaluation windows
  - Also triggers when derived `tenant_onboarding_milestones` hasn’t updated in > 60m
- **Step drop-off spike**
  - Triggers when a step’s completion rate drops > 20% vs trailing 7d baseline (configurable)
  - Default scope is Hosted API + self-serve cohorts; enterprise/dedicated cohorts can be configured separately

## Data sources and metric definitions
This pack assumes the following tables/views exist in the central analytics workspace (names match the platform telemetry spec as of March 2025):
- `analytics.onboarding_events_v1` (raw / lightly normalized events)
- `analytics.tenant_onboarding_milestones_v1` (derived; 1 row per tenant with timestamps per step)

Metric definitions are aligned to PM-751:
- TTFS: `first_success_at - signup_at`
  - `first_success_at` is the first gateway 2xx for the canonical generate/chat endpoints, excluding health checks
- TTFP: `first_prod_traffic_at - signup_at`
  - `first_prod_traffic_at` is computed by downstream classifier (>= N requests over M minutes OR explicit "prod" tag). The dashboard supports both modes via a variable.

Important: the dashboard does **not** join to billing for these KPIs (to avoid the known usage dashboard lag issue). We only use billing-derived fields when available as optional breakdown dimensions.

## How to install
- `obs-pack apply onboarding-kpis`
- or import the JSON from `packs/onboarding-kpis/dashboards/onboarding_kpis_grafana.json`

After import, set variables:
- `environment` (prod/staging)
- `cohort_window` (default: last 8 weeks)
- `deployment_mode` (default: hosted)

## Screenshots
Attached in internal PR UI (Grafana panels: funnel + TTFS/TTFP timeseries + alert preview).

## Notes / limitations
- Step drop-off alerting uses a simple baseline comparison. This is intentionally conservative for MVP; we can add seasonality-aware detection once we see stable weekly traffic.
- `sdk_language` depends on OpenAI-compat user-agent tagging (redwood-openai-compat#265). If not present, values show as `unknown`.
- This PR ships Hosted API defaults; Dedicated/Private filters are stubbed but not surfaced in the default dashboard nav.

## Testing
- Validated queries against staging analytics (last 14d) and ensured panels render with empty-state handling.
- Dry-run the alert rules with `alerts.test_mode=true` to confirm routing/labels.
- Checked for cardinality regressions (no per-tenant series in alerts; tenant-level only in dashboard table panels).

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind pack version bump only (no automatic enablement).
- SRE/Telemetry will enable alerts for internal dogfood cohorts first (see ENG-5815).

## Checklist
- [x] Dashboard uses standard variable names and folder layout
- [x] Alerts are disabled by default and include runbook links
- [x] No PII fields referenced (tenant_id is only used in table panels and not emitted in alert labels)
- [x] Added docs for metric definitions and known caveats

title: Fix timeout handling in telemetry queries (retries/backoff + better degraded-mode messaging)
preview:
Context: During incidents we often burst telemetry queries (GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler backpressure). When telemetry is slow or partially degraded, the bot currently either (a) times out and shows empty cards, or (b) holds the Slack interaction long enough that users think the bot is broken. This came up in INT-3341 and in recent #eng-infra / #eng-sre threads about bounding diagnostics load.

What this PR changes: 1) Adds a hard end-to-end deadline for telemetry collection per card render (default 4.5s, configurable), with per-query timeouts and cancellation propagation so we don’t keep hammering telemetry after the user path has moved on. 2) Implements bounded retries with exponential backoff + jitter for retryable classes (timeouts, 429, 502/503/504). Retries are capped (max 2) and respect the overall deadline. 3) Improves degraded-mode messaging in Slack cards when telemetry is missing/partial: instead of an empty card, we show a short reason (e.g. “Telemetry query timed out after 4.5s”) and immediately provide deep links to the relevant dashboard panels for manual drill-down. 4) Adds structured error codes in the incident context payload so downstream renderers/clients can consistently display failures (TIMEOUT, RATE_LIMITED, UPSTREAM_UNAVAILABLE, PERMISSION_DENIED, INVALID_QUERY). 5) Adds metrics and logs around retry/timeout behavior to validate we’re reducing empty-card incidence without increasing query load.

Behavior details: - Retry policy: retry only when status in {429, 502, 503, 504} or error is timeout / transient network error; do not retry on 400/401/403 or schema errors. - Backoff: base 150ms, factor 2.0, full jitter, capped at 900ms. - Deadline: total time budget includes retries; once the deadline is exceeded we return partial results + a degraded-mode banner, and emit a single structured error per missing signal group. - Slack UX: degraded-mode banner is intentionally short and does not include raw upstream error strings; we include a “View dashboards” button with pre-parameterized links (service/region/time window) and a “Copy manual query” action for on-call.

Testing: - Unit tests for retry classification and budget enforcement. - Integration test using the fake telemetry gateway to simulate a slow upstream and ensure we render partial results + banner. - Updated e2e to assert the message shown when telemetry times out.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Hook this into the telemetry query cache/coalescing work (ENG-5427) once that ships broadly, to avoid double-counting retries in request coalescing metrics. - Align the timeout budget with the perf canary thresholds once perf-canary/pr-221 is fully deployed.

Checklist: - [x] Added metrics: incident_bot.telemetry_query.duration_ms, incident_bot.telemetry_query.timeout, incident_bot.telemetry_query.retries, incident_bot.telemetry_query.degraded_mode - [x] Added log fields: incident_id, thread_ts, card_type, region, tenant_scope - [x] Docs: updated internal operator notes in the repo README section for diagnostics troubleshooting - [x] No customer data included in error strings; error messages are safe for #incidents / #eng-oncall

title: Publish baseline vs quant-profile cost/token + latency deltas (CSV summaries + artifacts)
preview:
Context: As part of the Quantization Profile Library rollout, we need a repeatable, reviewable set of benchmark outputs that quantify the impact of enabling a published quantization profile vs baseline precision on (a) unit economics (approx cost/token) and (b) latency/throughput across supported GPU SKUs. This PR adds a small publishing pipeline in benchmark-lab that generates normalized CSV summaries per model/profile/GPU and uploads both raw run logs and the rolled-up tables as CI artifacts. The goal is to let Applied ML + Runtime + Platform point to a single source of truth for “expected deltas” used by Console previews, rollout gates, and incident triage.

What’s included: (1) A new runner entrypoint that executes baseline + profile-enabled scenarios back-to-back with consistent warmup and pinned runtime build/commit; (2) CSV summaries under artifacts/quant_profiles/ summarizing p50/p95 end-to-end latency, decode latency, tokens/sec, VRAM peak, and derived cost/token; (3) A stable “schema_version” for the CSV format plus embedded metadata columns (model_id, variant, profile_id, profile_version, gpu_sku, runtime_commit, run_id, timestamp); (4) A README documenting how to reproduce locally and how to interpret cost/token.

Cost/token methodology: We compute cost/token as (gpu_hourly_usd / tokens_per_hour). gpu_hourly_usd comes from a static price table in this repo (documented as “estimates for comparison only”, not customer billing). tokens_per_hour is taken from measured tokens/sec after warmup and excluding initial prefill window. This is meant for relative deltas between baseline and profile-enabled on the same hardware + workload; absolute numbers will vary by fleet utilization and batching.

Workloads: This PR publishes deltas for the standard quant-profile perf suite workloads (short chat, long-context chat, and tool-call shaped prompts) at fixed concurrency points (1, 4, 16) for A100-80GB, H100-80GB, and L40S-48GB. Models currently included are the first beta targets (internal IDs): llama3.1-8b-instruct (x2), llama3.1-70b-instruct (x1), and qwen2.5-32b-instruct (x1) where profiles exist.

Example excerpt (see full CSV artifacts):
- llama3.1-8b-instruct:x2 INT8 profile (A100-80GB, conc=16, long-context): tokens/sec +22.8%, p95 e2e latency -6.1%, derived cost/token -18.6%
- llama3.1-70b-instruct:x1 FP8 mixed profile (H100-80GB, conc=4, short chat): tokens/sec +15.2%, p95 e2e latency -3.4%, derived cost/token -12.9%
- qwen2.5-32b-instruct:x1 INT8 profile (L40S-48GB, conc=16, tool-call): tokens/sec +9.7%, p95 e2e latency +2.1% (tail sensitivity), derived cost/token -7.8% (flagged for follow-up; see notes in README)

Notes / guardrails: This PR does not change any production defaults. Benchmarks are informational and intended to help validate expected savings and catch perf regressions. Profile compatibility is assumed pre-validated; this runner will fail fast if the runtime reports fallback or incompatibility (we export fallback_rate and enforce it == 0 for benchmark runs).

Follow-ups: (a) Add a lightweight “trend” job that compares latest results vs previous main commit and comments on PRs that touch runtime quantization paths; (b) Expand model coverage as new profiles are published; (c) Optionally export a JSON snapshot for Console preview ingestion once the Console team finalizes the ingestion path.

Screenshots/artifacts: CI run for this PR uploads: raw_run_logs.tgz, quant_profile_summary.csv, quant_profile_summary_by_gpu.csv, and per-model CSVs under artifacts/quant_profiles/<model_id>/.

Risk: Low. Changes are isolated to benchmark-lab scripts and CI. No customer-facing behavior change.

Refs: Confluence perf suite doc (quantization-profile-perf-benchmark-suite), runtime metrics naming from redwood/pr-18512, and rollout requirements from prd-quantization-profile-library-opt-in.

title: Include authz decision context in SIEM export (ECS/CIM mapping)
preview:
Summary
Adds support for exporting RBAC v2 authorization decision context fields (subject/action/resource/scope/policy_version/decision/denial reasons) through the audit-log-exporter and maps them to common SIEM schema conventions.

This PR is intended to unblock enterprise security questionnaire asks and pilot customer requirements where SIEM rules need to distinguish auth failures (deny) from application errors, and correlate access decisions with specific permission strings.

Context
- Redwood platform services are adding authz decision fields to audit log events (see redwood monorepo PR: redwood#28292).
- RBAC v2 requires evidence-friendly audit trails (SOC2 + enterprise questionnaires).
- Customers commonly ingest our audit logs into Splunk (CIM) and/or Elastic (ECS). They want stable field names, consistent typing, and predictable nullability.

What changed
1) New exported fields (non-breaking)
The exporter now forwards and normalizes these authz decision fields when present in the source event:
- authz.subject.id
- authz.subject.type (user | service_account | api_key | unknown)
- authz.subject.org_id
- authz.action (permission string, e.g., "org.api_keys.rotate")
- authz.resource.type (org | project | environment | deployment | cluster | api_key | audit_log_export | unknown)
- authz.resource.id (when available)
- authz.scope (canonical string representation: org:<id>[/project:<id>[/env:<id>]]; cluster scoped adds "/cluster:<id>")
- authz.decision (allow | deny)
- authz.denial_reason_codes (list of stable machine codes)
- authz.denial_message (optional, admin-only text; exporter defaults to NOT exporting free-form denial messages unless explicitly enabled)
- authz.policy.version (string)
- authz.policy.evaluator ("embedded" | "central" | "unknown")

2) SIEM schema mapping
Adds two output mappers:
- Elastic ECS-ish mapping (default for JSON output):
  - event.action = authz.action (when present)
  - event.outcome = success|failure (mapped from authz.decision)
  - user.id / user.type populated from authz.subject when the event actor is a human user
  - service.name = "redwood-audit-log-exporter"
  - labels.redwood_authz_policy_version, labels.redwood_scope (string labels for searchability)
  - tags includes "authz" when authz context exists

- Splunk CIM-friendly mapping (opt-in, for HEC payloads):
  - action = authz.action
  - status = allowed|denied
  - user / src_user populated when available
  - vendor_product = "Redwood Inference"
  - additional CIM-like fields namespaced under redwood.* to avoid collisions

3) Redaction / safety controls
- Denial message exporting is disabled by default (to avoid leaking internal policy evaluation details into customer SIEMs).
- Denial reason codes are exported as a list of stable codes only.
- Adds config flag: EXPORT_AUTHZ_DENIAL_MESSAGE=true to include authz.denial_message (intended for internal dogfood or Private installs where customers explicitly request it).

4) Compatibility behavior
- If upstream events do not yet contain authz context, exporter behavior is unchanged.
- If upstream events contain partial authz context, exporter exports what is present and avoids inventing data.
- No schema version bump required; this is additive.

Implementation notes
- Adds a small normalization layer in the pipeline that:
  - validates decision enums (defaults to "unknown" behavior by omitting outcome mapping)
  - coerces denial_reason_codes to [] if null
  - canonicalizes scope formatting to a stable string
- Ensures stable field types across outputs:
  - denial_reason_codes is always an array in JSON output
  - policy.version is always a string when present

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - JSON mapping outputs for allow and deny decisions
  - Splunk HEC payload formatting with and without authz fields
  - redaction behavior for denial_message
- Golden fixtures:
  - testdata/events/authz_allow.json
  - testdata/events/authz_deny.json
  - testdata/expected/ecs_allow.json
  - testdata/expected/ecs_deny.json
  - testdata/expected/splunk_hec_deny.json

Example output (JSON / ECS-ish)
Input event (abridged): actor=user:123 action=org.api_keys.rotate decision=deny reasons=["MISSING_PERMISSION","SCOPE_MISMATCH"] policy_version="rbac-bundle@2025-03-14.1"

Exported record (abridged):
- event.action: "org.api_keys.rotate"
- event.outcome: "failure"
- user.id: "123"
- labels.redwood_authz_policy_version: "rbac-bundle@2025-03-14.1"
- redwood.authz.decision: "deny"
- redwood.authz.denial_reason_codes: ["MISSING_PERMISSION","SCOPE_MISMATCH"]

Files / areas changed
- src/pipeline/normalize_authz.ts (new)
- src/mappers/ecs.ts
- src/mappers/splunk_cim.ts
- src/config.ts (new flags)
- src/types/audit_event.ts (authz field typing)
- test/mappers/ecs.test.ts
- test/mappers/splunk_cim.test.ts
- testdata/events/*
- testdata/expected/*
- README.md (configuration + field reference)

Commits (squash summary)

text:
Summary
Adds support for exporting RBAC v2 authorization decision context fields (subject/action/resource/scope/policy_version/decision/denial reasons) through the audit-log-exporter and maps them to common SIEM schema conventions.

This PR is intended to unblock enterprise security questionnaire asks and pilot customer requirements where SIEM rules need to distinguish auth failures (deny) from application errors, and correlate access decisions with specific permission strings.

Context
- Redwood platform services are adding authz decision fields to audit log events (see redwood monorepo PR: redwood#28292).
- RBAC v2 requires evidence-friendly audit trails (SOC2 + enterprise questionnaires).
- Customers commonly ingest our audit logs into Splunk (CIM) and/or Elastic (ECS). They want stable field names, consistent typing, and predictable nullability.

What changed
1) New exported fields (non-breaking)
The exporter now forwards and normalizes these authz decision fields when present in the source event:
- authz.subject.id
- authz.subject.type (user | service_account | api_key | unknown)
- authz.subject.org_id
- authz.action (permission string, e.g., "org.api_keys.rotate")
- authz.resource.type (org | project | environment | deployment | cluster | api_key | audit_log_export | unknown)
- authz.resource.id (when available)
- authz.scope (canonical string representation: org:<id>[/project:<id>[/env:<id>]]; cluster scoped adds "/cluster:<id>")
- authz.decision (allow | deny)
- authz.denial_reason_codes (list of stable machine codes)
- authz.denial_message (optional, admin-only text; exporter defaults to NOT exporting free-form denial messages unless explicitly enabled)
- authz.policy.version (string)
- authz.policy.evaluator ("embedded" | "central" | "unknown")

2) SIEM schema mapping
Adds two output mappers:
- Elastic ECS-ish mapping (default for JSON output):
  - event.action = authz.action (when present)
  - event.outcome = success|failure (mapped from authz.decision)
  - user.id / user.type populated from authz.subject when the event actor is a human user
  - service.name = "redwood-audit-log-exporter"
  - labels.redwood_authz_policy_version, labels.redwood_scope (string labels for searchability)
  - tags includes "authz" when authz context exists

- Splunk CIM-friendly mapping (opt-in, for HEC payloads):
  - action = authz.action
  - status = allowed|denied
  - user / src_user populated when available
  - vendor_product = "Redwood Inference"
  - additional CIM-like fields namespaced under redwood.* to avoid collisions

3) Redaction / safety controls
- Denial message exporting is disabled by default (to avoid leaking internal policy evaluation details into customer SIEMs).
- Denial reason codes are exported as a list of stable codes only.
- Adds config flag: EXPORT_AUTHZ_DENIAL_MESSAGE=true to include authz.denial_message (intended for internal dogfood or Private installs where customers explicitly request it).

4) Compatibility behavior
- If upstream events do not yet contain authz context, exporter behavior is unchanged.
- If upstream events contain partial authz context, exporter exports what is present and avoids inventing data.
- No schema version bump required; this is additive.

Implementation notes
- Adds a small normalization layer in the pipeline that:
  - validates decision enums (defaults to "unknown" behavior by omitting outcome mapping)
  - coerces denial_reason_codes to [] if null
  - canonicalizes scope formatting to a stable string
- Ensures stable field types across outputs:
  - denial_reason_codes is always an array in JSON output
  - policy.version is always a string when present

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - JSON mapping outputs for allow and deny decisions
  - Splunk HEC payload formatting with and without authz fields
  - redaction behavior for denial_message
- Golden fixtures:
  - testdata/events/authz_allow.json
  - testdata/events/authz_deny.json
  - testdata/expected/ecs_allow.json
  - testdata/expected/ecs_deny.json
  - testdata/expected/splunk_hec_deny.json

Example output (JSON / ECS-ish)
Input event (abridged): actor=user:123 action=org.api_keys.rotate decision=deny reasons=["MISSING_PERMISSION","SCOPE_MISMATCH"] policy_version="rbac-bundle@2025-03-14.1"

Exported record (abridged):
- event.action: "org.api_keys.rotate"
- event.outcome: "failure"
- user.id: "123"
- labels.redwood_authz_policy_version: "rbac-bundle@2025-03-14.1"
- redwood.authz.decision: "deny"
- redwood.authz.denial_reason_codes: ["MISSING_PERMISSION","SCOPE_MISMATCH"]

Files / areas changed
- src/pipeline/normalize_authz.ts (new)
- src/mappers/ecs.ts
- src/mappers/splunk_cim.ts
- src/config.ts (new flags)
- src/types/audit_event.ts (authz field typing)
- test/mappers/ecs.test.ts
- test/mappers/splunk_cim.test.ts
- testdata/events/*
- testdata/expected/*
- README.md (configuration + field reference)

3. mappers: add ECS-ish mapping for authz fields
4. mappers: add Splunk CIM mapping + HEC payload keys
5. config: add EXPORT_AUTHZ_DENIAL_MESSAGE flag (default false)
6. tests: fixtures + golden tests for allow/deny cases
7. docs: document new fields + schema mapping notes

Review notes / discussion highlights
- Marcus Lin: requested denial_message be off by default and that we prefer reason codes. Implemented.
- Allison Grant: asked to keep existing top-level fields stable and avoid renaming legacy actor fields. Confirmed additive only; legacy actor.* remains unchanged.
- Logan Wright: suggested always emitting arrays for list fields to avoid schema drift in SIEM; updated normalization to force [] instead of null.

CI

- unit: pass
- integration (splunk hec smoke): pass

- No customer-visible change until upstream events include authz context.

title: Tag eval outputs with experiment variant identifiers + publish compact score stream for Console
preview:
Motivation: Route-level A/B testing in Console needs a quality signal per {route, experiment_id, variant_id}. Today, eval-harness produces per-run artifacts (full JSON + HTML reports) but those are not consistently attributable to a routing experiment variant, and they’re too heavy for Console ingestion. This PR (1) propagates experiment attribution fields into eval execution and output artifacts, and (2) publishes a compact score stream suitable for near-real-time aggregation and dashboarding.

What’s included: (A) Variant tagging: add optional experiment context to eval runs, including experiment_id, variant_id, route_id, and a stable stickiness_key_hash (when provided), and persist these tags into (i) run metadata.json, (ii) per-prompt result rows, and (iii) summary.json. (B) Compact score stream: emit one record per {run_id, prompt_set_id, scorer_name} with normalized score, raw score, and minimal dimensions. This is designed to be ingested by Telemetry/Analytics and joined with experiment guardrails results without pulling full eval artifacts.

Schema (score stream v1): Each record is JSONL with fields: event_type=eval_score_v1, emitted_at, run_id, eval_suite_id, prompt_set_id, scorer_name, score_value, score_value_normalized, normalization_version, sample_n, model_id, model_revision, region (if applicable), route_id (optional), experiment_id (optional), variant_id (optional), attribution_source (router|manual|unknown), and trace_id (optional). Notes: (1) experiment_id/variant_id are omitted (not null) when not available to reduce payload size and simplify downstream cardinality controls; (2) scorer_name is restricted to an allowlist configured in eval-harness to prevent uncontrolled dimensionality.

Integration points: (1) CLI: new flags --experiment-id, --variant-id, --route-id, --attribution-source. (2) Runner API: EvalRunContext gains optional ExperimentAttribution. (3) Publishers: new ScoreStreamPublisher (default off) that can write to stdout (dev), file (integration tests), or the internal bus topic eval-score-stream-v1 (prod). The publisher is behind a config gate (EVAL_SCORE_STREAM_ENABLED) and can be enabled per environment.

Safety/rollout: Backward compatible. Existing artifact formats remain valid; we only add new keys. The score stream publisher is disabled by default. Added guardrails to avoid emitting records when scorer output is missing/NaN. Added sampling controls for very large prompt sets: emit summary scores always; per-prompt scores remain only in artifacts, not in the stream.

Testing: Added unit tests for attribution propagation, JSON schema validation tests for stream records, and an end-to-end fixture that runs a tiny suite and asserts that emitted JSONL contains correct experiment_id/variant_id. Also added a compatibility test to ensure older consumers parsing metadata.json don’t break.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (1) finalize Console-facing ‘quality’ metric mapping and thresholds (Applied ML + Product); (2) telemetry aggregation jobs (ENG-9027) to roll up score stream by {experiment_id, variant_id}; (3) documentation updates for customers/internal operators on how evals attach to experiments.

Risk notes: Cardinality is controlled by keeping the stream to a small dimension set; do not add raw prompt text or user identifiers. stickiness_key_hash is salted and truncated to prevent correlation across customers while allowing debugging for a single run.
