title: Add Dedicated staged rollout dashboards + alert templates (stage p95/p99, error rate, GPU saturation, promotion annotations)
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a first cut of dashboard + alert templates for the Dedicated staged rollout system. The dashboards are stage-aware (stage_id / stage_name) and are designed to support the rollout gate model in the policy engine (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health proxies). It also adds annotations for stage promotion/abort/hold events so operators can correlate metric shifts with rollout actions.

Context / dependencies: This is intended to ship alongside the redwood monorepo changes that (a) emit stage tags in metrics/traces and (b) provide audit events for promote/abort/hold. Dashboards assume the following labels are present on the relevant metrics: dedicated_pool_id, rollout_plan_id, stage_id, stage_name, region. For cardinality safety, the dashboards intentionally avoid any per-request labels and only group-by the allowlisted stage identifiers.

What’s included:
- Grafana dashboards (templated) for Dedicated staged rollouts:
  1) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Overview" (default landing)
     - Stage timeline annotations (promote/abort/hold)
     - p95/p99 latency per stage (overall + per region)
     - request rate + error rate (5xx + 429 separately)
     - saturation/headroom proxy panels (GPU utilization, queue depth)
     - OOM / throttling proxy panels (where available)
  2) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Drilldown" (single stage focus)
     - gating window panels (5m + 15m, side-by-side)
     - sample size panel (req/min, tokens/sec) to reduce false confidence
     - region comparison table with pass/fail indicator driven by threshold variables
  3) "Dedicated Rollouts: Fleet Signals" (operator/SRE)
     - node/GPU health summary by pool + region
     - ECC error rate proxy + throttling counters (when present)
- Alert templates (Prometheus rules) aligned to v1 gates:
  - GateCandidateHighErrorRate (per stage)
  - GateCandidateP99LatencyRegression (per stage)
  - GateCandidateHigh429Rate (per stage; motivated by early canary starving capacity)
  - GateCandidateGPUSaturation (per stage + per region)
  - GateCandidateQueueDepthSpike (per stage)
  - TelemetryStaleForStage (marks "unknown" gate evidence scenarios; ticket-level)
- Annotations:
  - Adds Grafana annotation queries backed by the audit event stream (via Loki index) for: rollout.plan.created, rollout.stage.promoted, rollout.stage.aborted, rollout.stage.held, rollout.stage.force_promoted.
  - Annotation text includes actor + reason (if provided) and links back to the Console rollout page (URL templated).

Notes on thresholds (v1):
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative and are parameterized as Grafana variables where possible so we can tune by tier without re-cutting dashboards.
- We split 429 from 5xx because 429 is the most common early-stage failure signature when partition sizing is too small.
- Missing data: dashboards show "no data" explicitly; alerts include a separate TelemetryStaleForStage rule instead of failing other rules open/closed.

Validation:
- Tested with staging data using synthetic stage tags and replayed a known canary incident window from SUP-28522 to confirm panels highlight 429 + queue depth.
- Verified annotations render correctly with sample audit events and do not explode cardinality.

Rollout plan:
- Land dashboards behind the dedicated_rollouts folder; do not set as default home.
- SRE to enable alert rules for staging first, then production after one week of tuning.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add a customer-facing pared-down dashboard variant (hides infra-only signals).
- Add quality regression panels (eval scores) once the stage-tagged eval pipeline is finalized.
- Tighten region impairment visualization (heatmap) after we confirm multi-region rollout behavior in beta.

Screenshots (staging):
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-overview
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-drilldown

References:
- Linear: ENG-5966 (stage-tagged metrics), PM-827 (default thresholds)
- Support: SUP-28522 (canary 429s), SUP-28605 (missing gate evidence)
- Related code: redwood PRs for stage tags + audit events (see internal rollout doc for exact SHAs).

title: Telemetry: cardinality guardrails for unit economics tags
preview:
Summary
- Adds hard validation + enforcement guardrails for high-cardinality tag dimensions used by the Unit Economics pipeline (model_id/model_version, region, customer_tier, quantization_profile, route_group, streaming).
- Prevents accidental explosions in metrics cardinality (e.g. route_id, raw customer identifiers, arbitrary quantization profile strings) that have previously caused ingestion lag and warehouse view instability.
- Emits explicit counters for dropped/rewritten tags so we can alert before dashboard coverage degrades.

Context
The internal Unit Economics dashboard depends on stable metering dimensions (see Confluence: token-metering-dimensions-and-cardinality). Over the last few weeks we’ve seen several near-misses where a single deploy introduced unbounded values in a tag (route id with embedded customer UUID; quantization profile leaking experiment run ids), which then:
- increased metrics series count significantly,
- delayed downstream rollups, and
- made hourly aggregates partially missing.

This PR implements guardrails at the telemetry emission boundary for the economics-related meter so we fail “safe” (map to canonical buckets) rather than “fail noisy” (explode series / drop ingestion).

Design (what changed)
1) Central TagPolicy and enforcement library
- Introduces telemetry/tag_guardrails (shared lib used by gateway + runtime meter emitter).
- Policy applied per dimension key:
  - max_value_length (truncate + mark)
  - allowed_values allowlist (optional)
  - allowed_regex (optional)
  - max_unique_values_per_window (per-process unique tracker; enforces using LRU+TTL)
  - fallback_value (what we emit when invalid / over limit)

2) Canonicalization changes for known offenders
- route_id is no longer emitted as a metric tag for unit economics. We emit route_group instead.
  - route_group is derived from route metadata (stable, low cardinality) and defaults to "unknown".
  - For debugging, the full route_id is still available in traces/logs (NOT as a metrics tag).
- quantization_profile is validated against a fixed set of allowed values (from model metadata config) and normalized (e.g. "int4-awq" and "INT4_AWQ" -> "int4_awq"). Unknown values map to "unknown".

3) Visibility / safety knobs
- New metrics:
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.rewritten_total{meter,dimension,reason}
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.dropped_total{meter,dimension,reason}
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.unique_limit_exceeded_total{meter,dimension}
- New feature flag:
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.unit_econ_v1
    - off: no guardrails
    - shadow: validate + emit counters, but do not rewrite/drop
    - enforce: rewrite/drop per policy

4) Aggregation compatibility
- Warehouse rollups already treat "unknown" as a valid bucket. This PR standardizes the fallback values and updates the dimension dictionary in-code comments.

Rollout plan
- Land behind feature flag in shadow mode (24h) in prod.
- SRE/Observability to review rewritten/dropped counters and confirm no unexpected churn.
- Flip to enforce during a low-traffic window.

Testing
- Unit tests for TagPolicy (regex/allowlist/length/unique-window behavior).
- Integration-style tests for the unit-econ meter emitter (ensures we never emit disallowed dimensions; ensures stable fallback values).

Notes / follow-ups
- Follow-up PR will wire guardrail counters into the unit econ pipeline Grafana pack and add an alert when rewritten_total exceeds threshold for >15m (tracked in ENG-8429).

Risk assessment
- Low product risk: only affects internal unit-econ metering tags.
- Moderate observability risk if we incorrectly bucket a tag; mitigated via shadow-mode + metrics.

Screenshots
- N/A (backend only)

Changelog
- Internal-only telemetry behavior change; no external API impact.

title: Runtime: load quantization profile artifacts, apply mode, and emit observability signals
preview: Summary: This PR wires the serving runtime to (1) consume the resolved quantization profile reference from routing metadata, (2) fetch and validate the corresponding profile metadata + artifacts from the model registry, (3) apply quantization mode deterministically for the request/model shard, and (4) emit metrics + structured events so rollouts can be observed and debugged. Motivation: The Quantization Profile Library is opt-in and route-scoped. For v1 we need deterministic selection, safe fallback, and clear signals for SRE and customers to verify whether a profile was applied for a given request. This PR is scoped to runtime behavior only; control plane resolution/compatibility checks are handled upstream (see related PRs). Design overview: - Input contract: router attaches quantization selection metadata to the request context (grpc metadata + internal request proto field). Runtime treats this as advisory and re-validates profile/model compatibility against the local model + GPU runtime capabilities. - Profile loading: new QuantProfileManager loads profile metadata via registry client (cached), then downloads artifact bundle (signed) to a local content-addressed cache. Artifacts are keyed by (profile_id, artifact_sha256) to guarantee determinism and avoid alias drift. - Deterministic application: on each request, runtime resolves outcome = {disabled|applied|fallback} with a single pass decision. If artifacts are missing/invalid/incompatible, runtime falls back to baseline precision for that model variant and records the reason code. No retry loops. - Runtime integration: ModelExecutor now accepts an optional QuantizationModeSpec derived from the profile. Kernel selection remains in kernel auto-selector; runtime passes a constrained spec and relies on kernel guardrails to reject unsupported mixes. - Observability: Adds counters and histograms for enablement outcome, artifact cache behavior, load latencies, and per-request annotations (logs + traces) including quant_profile_id and resolution outcome. Metrics are low-cardinality by design; profile_id is recorded in logs/traces but not as a metrics label. title: Dedicated: tier-aware admission control guards (queue caps + overload shedding)
preview:
Context

Dedicated throughput guarantees require us to keep tail latency and per-deployment throughput stable even when a pool is oversubscribed (mis-sized customers, sudden bursts, noisy neighbors, or control-plane misroutes). Today, overload behavior is mostly “best effort” and can lead to queue buildup that drags the entire pool down.

This PR implements tier-aware admission control guards in the gateway->orchestrator path:
- queue caps per (deployment_id, route) with tier-specific ceilings
- overload shedding rules that preferentially shed lower-tier/burst traffic while protecting committed Dedicated throughput floors
- consistent, observable rejection semantics (429 vs 503) and reason codes

Links
- Linear: ENG-19257 (Tier-aware admission control)
- Related: ENG-19244 / PR-19321 (per-deployment throughput metrics), ENG-19263 / PR-19366 (tier->pool mapping)
- Support signal: SUP-18422 (customer-reported throughput below commitment during saturation)

What changed

1) Admission control policy (tier-aware)
- Introduced AdmissionPolicyEvaluator with inputs: tier, workload class (dedicated_committed vs dedicated_burst), route_type (chat/embeddings/rerank), and pool saturation signals.
- Default queue caps (configurable per region/pool):
  - Standard: smaller per-deployment queue cap, more aggressive shedding when pool_saturation > threshold
  - Plus: medium queue cap, protected longer before shedding
  - Premium: largest queue cap, last to shed; attempts soft-failover to warm pool if enabled (still feature-flagged)
- Added “burst lane” concept: burst traffic is accepted only if pool has headroom; otherwise shed with explicit reason=burst_over_capacity.

2) Overload shedding behavior
- When pool enters overload mode, shed in this order:
  (a) burst traffic (all tiers)
  (b) Standard committed (only after sustained overload window)
  (c) Plus committed (only if overload persists past second window)
  (d) Premium committed (never proactively shed; only reject if deployment queue cap is hit)
- To avoid starvation, implemented a small “min-admit” token for each committed deployment per interval (configurable) so one noisy tenant can’t fully block others.

3) Response semantics
- 429 Too Many Requests: per-deployment queue cap exceeded OR customer-side concurrency cap exceeded (if enabled).
- 503 Service Unavailable: pool overload shed (explicitly indicates system-side capacity pressure).
- Added structured error fields: admission_reject_reason, tier, is_burst, pool_id, retry_after_ms.

4) Observability
- New metrics (all tagged by deployment_id, tier, pool_id, region, model where available):
  - admission_queue_depth (gauge)
  - admission_rejects_total{reason=queue_cap|pool_overload|burst_over_capacity|concurrency_cap}
  - admission_overload_mode (gauge)
  - admission_wait_ms (histogram)
- Added tracing span annotations on rejected requests for faster correlation in incident reviews.

5) Config + feature flagging
- Feature flag: dedicated.tier_admission_control (off by default).
- Config knobs (dynamic): per-tier queue caps, overload thresholds, min-admit rate, and burst headroom threshold.
- Safe fallback: if tier metadata missing, treat as Standard.

Changed areas / files (high level)
- services/gateway/admission/
  - policy_evaluator.ts
  - queue_manager.ts
  - reject_response.ts
- services/orchestrator/pool_saturation/
  - saturation_signals.ts
- packages/config/
  - dedicated_admission_control.yaml
- packages/telemetry/metrics/
  - admission_control_metrics.ts
- docs/internal/runbooks/
  - dedicated-tier-overload-behavior.md

Example snippet (illustrative)

- Reject payload now includes reason codes:
  {
    "error": "request_rejected",
    "status": 503,
    "admission_reject_reason": "pool_overload",
    "tier": "plus",
    "is_burst": false,
    "retry_after_ms": 250
  }

Test plan
- Unit tests:
  - policy evaluator tier ordering + shedding precedence
  - queue cap enforcement per deployment
  - reason code mapping to status codes
- Integration tests (simulated saturation):
  - ensure Premium committed maintains throughput/latency while Standard burst sheds first
  - ensure min-admit prevents starvation across committed deployments
- Load test (staging, us-east):
  - replay of SUP-18422 traffic pattern with synthetic burst; verified queue depth stays bounded and throughput floors regress less under contention

Rollout plan
- Enable in staging for 48 hours with synthetic overload + one real Dedicated canary.
- Gradually enable in prod behind feature flag for a small set of Dedicated customers (Standard+Plus first, Premium last).
- Monitor admission_rejects_total + throughput SLO panels; tune queue caps per pool based on observed variance.

Risks / notes
- This does not change token accounting; throughput metric fixes for streaming are tracked separately.
- Incorrect tier metadata would cause misclassification; added guardrails + logging and a default to Standard.

Reviewer notes / discussion
- Mina: requested explicit ordering + starvation protection; added min-admit and documented shedding order in runbook.
- Rafael: asked to treat overload shedding as SRE-page-worthy only when it impacts committed lanes; added separate metrics for is_burst and committed, and clarified in runbook how to alert.
- Aisha: requested reason code normalization and retry_after_ms; implemented in reject_response.ts.
- Cole: requested billing/metering alignment (burst vs committed); added is_burst tag + reason=burst_over_capacity to make downstream aggregation deterministic.

CI
- build: pass
- unit tests: pass
- lint: pass
- integration (staging): pass

Merge
- Squash merged to main (commit message: "Dedicated: tier-aware admission control guards").

title: Gateway: whitelist + validate quickstart template telemetry headers
preview:
Context: DevEx quickstart templates need a reliable, low-cardinality way to tag traffic as template-generated so we can measure activation funnel and time-to-first-success without relying on parsing user-agent strings. Today the API gateway drops unknown `x-*` headers by default; templates adding a telemetry header would silently lose that tag. This PR introduces an explicit allowlist in the gateway for a small set of template telemetry headers and performs strict validation to prevent abuse, high-cardinality explosions, and accidental PII leakage.

What this does: Adds a `template telemetry` header allowlist and validator at the gateway edge. Allowed headers are normalized into a structured internal context object that can be consumed by downstream logging/metrics/analytics (follow-up PRs). Invalid values are rejected with a 400 (clear error message) to avoid partial/ambiguous telemetry.

Allowed headers (v1): `x-redwood-template-id` (e.g. `rag-agent`), `x-redwood-template-version` (semver like `0.3.1`), `x-redwood-template-lang` (`python`|`typescript`), `x-redwood-template-origin` (`cli`|`github`), `x-redwood-template-otel` (`enabled`|`disabled`).

Validation rules: values must match `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]{0,63}$` (lowercase enforced), max length 64, no whitespace, no commas/quotes/newlines. `x-redwood-template-version` is additionally checked as semver-like `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` with an optional `-prerelease` suffix. Any header not in the allowlist continues to be stripped by default.

Security/privacy notes: These headers are intended to be low-cardinality and non-user-content. This PR does not accept arbitrary key/value tagging. We do not log raw request headers; we only log the validated, normalized fields. Review requested from Security and Observability to confirm cardinality + redaction posture.

Operational notes: This is required for the Quickstart Templates KPI dashboard (PM-912). Follow-up work will plumb these fields into usage analytics (Console filter work) and template repos will keep sending these headers behind an opt-in flag to avoid issues with restrictive corporate proxies.

Testing: Added unit tests for allowlist behavior, validation failures, normalization, and a regression test that unknown headers are still dropped.

title: Dedicated: emit burst-capacity metering events (committed vs burst) for billing exports
preview: Context: Dedicated tiers require billing/usage export to separate committed throughput consumption from burst capacity so Finance/Sales/CS can explain invoices and enforce tier policies (ENG-19302). This PR introduces a canonical metering event for burst usage, wires it into the request accounting path, and adds a daily aggregation job that produces committed_vs_burst totals per deployment_id/model/region. Definitions: (1) committed_usage_tokens = tokens attributed while the deployment is within its committed throughput floor capacity slice, (2) burst_usage_tokens = tokens attributed while the deployment consumes burst pool capacity above its committed slice, (3) unmetered_tokens = tokens excluded from billing exports (e.g., internal health checks, warmup). Attribution is computed at request finalization using the tier-aware admission/routing decision already attached to the request context (pool_assignment + capacity_class). Guardrails: feature flag `metering.dedicated_burst_v1` default off; if the capacity_class tag is missing, we fall back to committed (to avoid under-billing) and emit a counter `metering_burst_missing_capacity_class_total` for cleanup. Also includes unit tests for streaming vs non-streaming to ensure we do not double-count tokens, and adds a small schema version bump for the billing export (new columns) behind `export.schema_version >= 2025_02_25`. Rollout plan: enable flag for 2 internal Dedicated deployments, validate dashboards/export parity for 72h, then expand to beta customers selected by CS; Finance to validate sample invoices before GA.

title: Add NCCL collective counters and rank ids to TP tracing spans
preview:
Summary: Extend TP tracing instrumentation to include (a) rank identifiers and (b) NCCL collective counters on relevant spans so we can correlate “silent stalls” across ranks/replicas and disambiguate scheduler starvation vs comms waits. This is part of the ENG-4823 instrumentation work and complements the new heartbeat metrics by providing per-request, per-iteration context in traces.

Motivation / Background: During recent Dedicated incidents (see SUP-48377 and the 2025-02-17 Sev1 thread), we had cases where p99 latency spiked and requests timed out without a clear signal of whether we were stuck waiting on a collective, stuck on a CUDA event/stream wait, or simply starving collectives behind prefill-heavy work. Metrics help at fleet level, but when debugging a single wedged replica we need to answer: “Which collective were we on?”, “Did all ranks reach the same point?”, and “Are we accumulating in-flight collectives?” This PR adds stable, low-cardinality tracing attributes so we can align spans across ranks and quickly spot divergence.

What changed (high level): Add new tracing attributes to existing TP spans (prefill/decode iteration + comm boundary spans) and thread a small amount of TP context into tracing helpers.
- Add rank identifiers: tp.rank_id, tp.world_size, tp.tp_group_id (stable identifier for the TP group within a replica; not globally unique).
- Add NCCL counters: tp.collective.seq (monotonic per-rank sequence number), tp.collective.in_flight (gauge sampled at span start/end), tp.collective.op (enum-ish string: all_reduce/all_gather/reduce_scatter/broadcast), tp.collective.bytes (best-effort estimate).
- Add correlation attributes: tp.step_id (decode iteration / scheduler step number), tp.request_phase (prefill|decode), tp.replica_id (already present; now consistently attached to the child spans as well).

Implementation details: 1) Introduced a lightweight TpTraceContext struct (rank_id/world_size/tp_group_id) plumbed from the TP runtime init into the tracing layer. 2) NCCL wrappers now bump a per-rank atomic collective_seq and track in_flight via scoped increment/decrement. 3) Tracing helpers attach attributes only when a span is recording (OpenTelemetry span.isRecording()), and we avoid allocation-heavy formatting in the hot path (precomputed op name table; integer attributes where possible). 4) Added a guard knob: RUNTIME_TRACE_TP_COLLECTIVES=0/1 (default 1 when tracing enabled, but can be disabled for microbench runs).

Overhead / perf notes: We measured overhead in benchmark-lab on A100-80GB TP=4 with mixed prefill/decode (the same distribution used in the hang repro harness work). With tracing enabled and sampled at 1%, the additional attribute attachment increased end-to-end p50 by ~0.1–0.2% and did not move p99 outside noise; CPU overhead on the runtime thread was measurable in the profile but small (<0.5% on the instrumentation codepath). With tracing enabled and sampled at 100% (non-prod setting), overhead is larger (~1–2% p50) primarily due to exporter/SDK costs, not the attribute computations themselves.

Why attributes (not events): We want these fields queryable in our existing trace UI for joins/group-bys across spans. We purposely kept the attribute set low-cardinality: rank_id is bounded by world_size, collective.seq is monotonic but per-span value (not used as a metric label), and op is from a small fixed set.

Testing: - Unit tests for counter correctness and in_flight scoping (ensures in_flight never goes negative and returns to baseline even on error paths). - A small integration test that runs a fake TP group and asserts spans contain tp.rank_id and tp.collective.op when tracing is enabled. - Manual validation on a canary replica with NCCL debug disabled (INT-2219 guidance): verified trace attributes show rank divergence when injecting a fault (paired with the watchdog/failure tests work).

Rollout plan: - Merge behind the existing tracing enablement; safe to ship with runtime 1.19 canary. - If we observe any unexpected overhead in high-throughput Hosted, we can disable via RUNTIME_TRACE_TP_COLLECTIVES=0 without a rebuild.

Screenshots / Examples (trace query snippet): Example attributes on span "runtime.tp.decode_step": tp.rank_id=2 tp.world_size=4 tp.step_id=187 tp.collective.seq=913 tp.collective.in_flight=1 tp.collective.op=all_reduce tp.collective.bytes=4194304. In a stall case, we expect to see ranks disagree on tp.collective.seq or show in_flight>0 for an extended duration.

Follow-ups: - Once ENG-4823 heartbeat metrics land everywhere, we should add a trace/metric correlation note in the TP stall triage playbook (Confluence) to standardize debugging steps. - Consider adding a per-replica sampled “collective seq delta” log line on watchdog trigger to reduce dependency on traces when sampling is low.

What changed (high level): Add new tracing attributes to existing TP spans (prefill/decode iteration + comm boundary spans) and thread a small amount of TP context into tracing helpers.
- Add rank identifiers: tp.rank_id, tp.world_size, tp.tp_group_id (stable identifier for the TP group within a replica; not globally unique).
- Add NCCL counters: tp.collective.seq (monotonic per-rank sequence number), tp.collective.in_flight (gauge sampled at span start/end), tp.collective.op (enum-ish string: all_reduce/all_gather/reduce_scatter/broadcast), tp.collective.bytes (best-effort estimate).
- Add correlation attributes: tp.step_id (decode iteration / scheduler step number), tp.request_phase (prefill|decode), tp.replica_id (already present; now consistently attached to the child spans as well).

Implementation details: 1) Introduced a lightweight TpTraceContext struct (rank_id/world_size/tp_group_id) plumbed from the TP runtime init into the tracing layer. 2) NCCL wrappers now bump a per-rank atomic collective_seq and track in_flight via scoped increment/decrement. 3) Tracing helpers attach attributes only when a span is recording (OpenTelemetry span.isRecording()), and we avoid allocation-heavy formatting in the hot path (precomputed op name table; integer attributes where possible). 4) Added a guard knob: RUNTIME_TRACE_TP_COLLECTIVES=0/1 (default 1 when tracing enabled, but can be disabled for microbench runs).

Overhead / perf notes: We measured overhead in benchmark-lab on A100-80GB TP=4 with mixed prefill/decode (the same distribution used in the hang repro harness work). With tracing enabled and sampled at 1%, the additional attribute attachment increased end-to-end p50 by ~0.1–0.2% and did not move p99 outside noise; CPU overhead on the runtime thread was measurable in the profile but small (<0.5% on the instrumentation codepath). With tracing enabled and sampled at 100% (non-prod setting), overhead is larger (~1–2% p50) primarily due to exporter/SDK costs, not the attribute computations themselves.

Why attributes (not events): We want these fields queryable in our existing trace UI for joins/group-bys across spans. We purposely kept the attribute set low-cardinality: rank_id is bounded by world_size, collective.seq is monotonic but per-span value (not used as a metric label), and op is from a small fixed set.

Testing: - Unit tests for counter correctness and in_flight scoping (ensures in_flight never goes negative and returns to baseline even on error paths). - A small integration test that runs a fake TP group and asserts spans contain tp.rank_id and tp.collective.op when tracing is enabled. - Manual validation on a canary replica with NCCL debug disabled (INT-2219 guidance): verified trace attributes show rank divergence when injecting a fault (paired with the watchdog/failure tests work).

Rollout plan: - Merge behind the existing tracing enablement; safe to ship with runtime 1.19 canary. - If we observe any unexpected overhead in high-throughput Hosted, we can disable via RUNTIME_TRACE_TP_COLLECTIVES=0 without a rebuild.

Screenshots / Examples (trace query snippet): Example attributes on span "runtime.tp.decode_step": tp.rank_id=2 tp.world_size=4 tp.step_id=187 tp.collective.seq=913 tp.collective.in_flight=1 tp.collective.op=all_reduce tp.collective.bytes=4194304. In a stall case, we expect to see ranks disagree on tp.collective.seq or show in_flight>0 for an extended duration.

Follow-ups: - Once ENG-4823 heartbeat metrics land everywhere, we should add a trace/metric correlation note in the TP stall triage playbook (Confluence) to standardize debugging steps. - Consider adding a per-replica sampled “collective seq delta” log line on watchdog trigger to reduce dependency on traces when sampling is low.

title: Add saturation signal queries (GPU/queue/KV cache) to incident context payload
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new “Saturation signals” card to the incident-bot v2 context payload and Slack rendering. The card pulls a bounded set of telemetry signals for the affected service/region over a short window (default: last 15m) and summarizes them using simple thresholding so responders can quickly decide whether they’re dealing with capacity/scheduler pressure vs. an application/deploy regression.

Signals included (initial set): (1) GPU utilization (avg/p95), (2) scheduler/ingress queue depth (p95 + max), (3) KV cache pressure (p95), plus optional backpressure/admission-control indicators when present.

This is designed to be safe under incident conditions: strict timeouts, query budget enforcement, and an explicit degraded-mode response (cached snapshot + dashboard links) when live queries fail.

## Motivation
Multiple recent incidents (e.g., EU-west capacity saturation + streaming timeouts) required responders to manually piece together GPU/queue/KV cache data across dashboards. Our “first 5 minutes” goal is to provide consistent, actionable saturation context alongside deploy/config cards. This PR implements the telemetry integration portion for ENG-5423.

## What changed
- Adds a telemetry query client wrapper with:
  - hard timeout (2.0s total, 1 retry with jittered backoff)
  - bounded query time window (15m) and fixed aggregations
  - tag allowlist (service, region, pool, route where applicable) to avoid incident-bot creating high-cardinality queries
  - per-thread request coalescing hook (wired but cache layer comes in ENG-5427)
- Adds signal definitions + thresholds:
  - GPU util highlight when p95 >= 90% or avg >= 80%
  - Queue depth highlight when p95 >= 200 or max >= 500 (unit: requests waiting; see notes below)
  - KV cache pressure highlight when p95 >= 0.85 (fraction of configured KV cache capacity)
  - Each highlight includes “why it matters” copy and a suggested next step (e.g., check routing/fallback policy, batching changes, or regional capacity)
- Adds payload shape for `saturation` context and Slack rendering:
  - A compact summary line + 3–5 key metrics
  - A “View dashboard” deep link (templated) with service/region/time window
  - “Degraded mode” messaging when queries time out (no raw errors dumped into #incidents)

## Example payload (redacted)
The bot now attaches the following fields under the incident context payload (names may evolve; see inline comments for versioning):
`saturation.window=15m; saturation.gpu_util.avg=0.77; saturation.gpu_util.p95=0.91; saturation.queue_depth.p95=245; saturation.queue_depth.max=610; saturation.kv_cache_pressure.p95=0.88; saturation.highlights=[gpu_util_high, queue_depth_high, kv_pressure_high]; saturation.links.dashboard=https://console.redwood.example/obs/saturation?service=gateway&region=eu-west&from=-15m`

## Notes / guardrails
- Queue depth metric semantics differ slightly between Hosted vs Dedicated pools today; this PR uses the shared “requests waiting” gauge that telemetry team is standardizing. We only surface a single number for now and link to the dashboard for per-component breakdown.
- If region is unknown (e.g., incident declared before we have a region hint), the card falls back to “global” aggregation and labels the output accordingly.
- This PR does not introduce tenant-scoped queries; these are service/region/pool level signals only.

## Testing
- Unit tests for query builder (ensures allowlisted tags only, fixed aggregation/window)
- Snapshot tests for Slack blocks (normal + degraded mode + missing metric cases)
- Integration test against dev telemetry endpoint (skipped in CI; runnable locally)

## Rollout
- Feature-flag gated behind `incident_bot_v2_saturation_card` (enabled in staging only by default)
- Production rollout planned as part of ENG-5428 cohort beta

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5427: Add caching/request coalescing across threads and repeated interactions
- ENG-5429: Load-shedding policy for diagnostics during telemetry brownouts

Refs: ENG-5423; related: PR-1865 (timeout handling improvements) and observability-pack dashboards PR (forthcoming).

title: Unit economics: warehouse view performance optimizations (partitioning + query plan fixes)
preview: This PR improves drilldown query latency for the internal Unit Economics dashboard by tightening warehouse table layout (partitioning/clustering), removing a few high-cost joins and DISTINCTs in the hot path, and switching the most commonly-hit rollups to pre-aggregated/materialized views. The primary goal is to keep p95 query time for time-series + top-N breakdowns under ~2s for typical Finance/Product filter combinations (7d/30d, group_by=model|region|tier) while keeping hourly freshness and correctness intact. Context: during dogfood we saw a few breakdowns regress to 10–25s when grouping by model+region with additional filters (quantization_profile, streaming) due to full scans on hourly fact tables and an inefficient join to the rate card + region mapping tables. This is follow-on work to the initial aggregation job (PR #19342) and API endpoints (PR #19374). Key changes: (1) Re-partition hourly fact + rollup tables by usage_date (derived from usage_hour) and cluster by (canonical_region, customer_tier, model_id) to align with our most common predicates; (2) Introduce a slim dimension table with integer surrogate keys for model_id/region/tier to reduce row width in the largest fact table and avoid repeated string normalization; (3) Replace COUNT(DISTINCT request_id) and per-row JSON extraction in the dashboard views with precomputed columns emitted by the aggregation job; (4) Add/replace two materialized views: unit_econ_rollup_daily_mv (default for 7d/30d) and unit_econ_breakdown_topn_mv (serves top-N tables without scanning the full hourly set); (5) Rewrite the dashboard views to join rate card + allocation factors after aggregation (per hour/day bucket) rather than per request, and to avoid joining region normalization at query time (use canonical_region already stored). Expected impact (measured on staging warehouse with a 14-day slice): time_series(group_by=model, 30d) p95 12.8s -> 1.9s; topN_regions(7d, model=all) p95 8.4s -> 1.3s; drilldown model+region+tier (30d) p95 21.5s -> 2.6s. Notes: No changes to metric definitions; cost attribution math is unchanged. This PR includes a safe migration path: new tables/MVs are built side-by-side, views are swapped, and we keep the prior views for one release behind a feature flag for quick rollback. Backfill/refresh: materialized views refresh hourly; daily MV uses incremental refresh with a 3-day lookback to accommodate late-arriving telemetry.

title: Add canary check for output-token metering parity (streaming + non-streaming)
preview:
Context: we’ve had at least one production regression where streaming responses under-counted output tokens in metering, which then cascaded into Unit Economics dashboards (blended $/1M output tokens) and reconciliation views. This PR adds an automated canary that detects output-token metering regressions before a gateway/runtime change is widely rolled out.

What this adds:
- New canary suite `metering_output_token_parity` that runs against a small allowlisted canary tenant in prod.
- Exercises BOTH streaming and non-streaming generation paths (including tool-call shaped outputs) and validates:
  1) output_tokens reported by the API response metadata
  2) output_tokens recorded by metering events (usage pipeline)
  3) that the delta between (1) and (2) is within a configured tolerance after accounting for ingestion latency
- Adds guardrails for ingestion lag: the canary polls the metering lookup API for up to 6 minutes (configurable) before declaring missing events.
- Emits a single structured result event per run (pass/fail + diagnostics) and wires alert routing:
  - warning to #eng-platform when parity drift > 0.5% for a single run
  - page oncall (telemetry) when drift > 1.0% for 3 consecutive runs OR when missing metering events exceed 2% of requests

Key implementation details:
- Request set: 18 requests per run (9 streaming, 9 non-streaming) with fixed prompt fixtures to keep variance low.
- Models: uses `rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct` (cheap + stable) pinned to a specific model version for determinism.
- Tokenization alignment: uses the server-side token counts only (we do NOT attempt client-side tokenization); the canary is specifically checking parity between two server-side sources of truth.
- Correlation: uses `x-request-id` and `metering_correlation_id` to look up metering records; falls back to time window + route + model + canary tenant ID when correlation ID missing.
- Flake reduction: retries on 429/503 with exponential backoff; run is considered inconclusive (non-paging) if infra is degraded and error rate > 30%.

Rollout:
- Enabled in dry-run mode for 24h (records metrics but does not page).
- After 24h, flip `CANARY_METERING_PARITY_PAGING_ENABLED=true` in the perf-canary prod deployment.

Notes / limitations:
- This is not a billing reconciliation test. It only checks output token counts parity between API-side accounting and metering events.
- Known source of noise: very rare duplicate metering events; the canary de-dupes by (correlation_id, sequence).

Operational links:
- Runbook draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/unit-econ-output-token-parity (will be finalized alongside ENG-8435)

Why now:
- The internal Unit Economics dashboard (v1) depends on reliable output token counts (especially streaming); this canary is intended to catch regressions like the one fixed in `redwood` PR-19355 before they impact exec/finance reporting.

title: Add data quality checks + anomaly alerts for unit economics pipeline
preview:
Context: We’ve seen multiple cases where the Unit Economics dashboard showed sudden improvements or drops that were actually pipeline issues (most commonly ingestion lag or missing dimensions), e.g. the “hours missing” incident thread and earlier streaming token undercount investigations. This PR adds first-pass data quality checks and anomaly alerts for the hourly unit economics aggregation outputs so we can detect regressions quickly and avoid Finance/Product interpreting bad data.

What this PR does: (1) Completeness checks per hour: verifies presence of expected rows for core dimensions (region, customer_tier, model) and flags coverage below threshold; (2) Missing dimension checks: alerts when unknown/NULL region or customer_tier exceeds a configured percentage; (3) Token-volume anomaly checks: compares output_tokens to a trailing baseline (7d median by {region,tier} and global) and alerts on sudden drops/spikes; (4) Freshness checks: alerts if the latest successfully published hour is older than N minutes; (5) Emits standardized alert events to PagerDuty + Slack via our existing “observability-pack” sink, with runbook links and dashboards.

Notes/constraints: - These checks operate on the post-aggregation tables (warehouse) rather than raw metering to keep signal stable and avoid cardinality explosions. - Baselines are computed on “finance_view” (rate-card-stable) by default; “live_view” is available but not used for alerting to avoid false positives during backfills. - Thresholds are intentionally conservative in v1; tuning will happen after a week of dogfooding.

Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold logic and baseline computation edge cases (new region/tier appearing, backfill window overlaps). - Ran ad-hoc validation in staging by simulating missing tiers and forcing a token drop.

Rollout plan: Merge behind ECON_DQ_ALERTS_ENABLED flag default OFF in prod. SRE (Sean) will enable for internal dogfood after we observe 48h of clean staging behavior. Finance will be notified once alert noise is acceptable.

Follow-ups: - Add per-model-version anomaly checks once model_id normalization is fully rolled out everywhere. - Add explicit detection for streaming/non-streaming parity drift once the streaming metering canary is fully green for a full week.

Refs: ENG-8429, ENG-8430; incident context: “metric drop / hours missing” + internal backfill performance issues; runbook draft will land separately (ENG-8435).
